record.description: Ernest Golden grew up in Athens, Georgia where he started working from a young age. Golden recants his family background and his experiences with certain individuals that made an impact on his life. After the Pearl Harbor attack, Golden found himself making his way to Hawaii as a job opportunity arose. Once he arrived in Hawaii, Golden reminisces on his social experiences there, from the public housing discrimination he faced to his fun nights as a bachelor. He puts great emphasis on the civilian contributions made during WWII and justifies his fight to make those accomplishments known.
record.transcript:
DUNHAM: Okay, it's July 24, 2014, and we're grateful that Ernest Golden has invited us to his lovely home, here on the oceanfront of North Shore of Oahu. We're here with what's part of the Rosie the Riveter/World War II Home Front oral history project. It's our first interview, focusing on Hawaii during those years. But we're going to start at the beginning and go back. What we do is usually ask what is your full name and date of birth?
GOLDEN: My full name is Ernest Golden. I use middle initial L, for LaFaris Golden. Date of birth, May 21, 1923. What else do you want to know?
DUNHAM:Where were you born?
GOLDEN: I was born in Athens, Georgia. That's northeast of Atlanta, seventy-two miles northeast of Atlanta. I left Athens at the age of 19.
DUNHAM:Okay. Well, can you tell me a little bit about your family background? Did you know your grandparents, and do you know farther back in your family history, as well?
GOLDEN: I remember my life from the age of three. I recall my mother and I and my brother. I'm three years his senior. I recall that we lived in a little farmhouse, not far from my grandfather's home. My grandfather was a sharecropper. He had something like something like 3- to 500 acres of land that he was sharecropping, with a man we called Mr. Butler. From five years of age--. I am the oldest--or was the oldest--of several grandchildren and great-grandchildren. I grew up in my grandfather's house. My mother and father worked. Not on the farm but worked in town. My father was a truck driver. My mother worked in a laundry, in what we call uptown Athens, I guess. I recall so much love--being the oldest grandchild, and for quite some time, the only grandchild--in my grandfather's and grandmother's household. My grandfather was a Johnson. Lum Johnson. My grandmother was Mamie Johnson. I was fortunate enough to meet my great-grandmother, who was an ex-slave. We called her Grandma Laine, L-A-I-N-E, I think. I recall my grandmother Laine, who was a tall, mahogany-colored black woman, very slender. I recall that I would let my great-grandmother hold me, whereas my brother would not. This may have been the beginning of my ability to associate with so many people over the years, as time went by. I grew up with my two youngest uncles. My grandparents had three daughters--my mother, Viola; my Aunt Magnolia, who was next; and my Aunt Mary Ann. Her name's Mary Ann. My uncles in this order were the oldest Buster Johnson, the oldest brother; then Wayman Johnson, we called Bugg; Columbus Johnson, who died at the age of nineteen.
DUNHAM:May I ask how Columbus Johnson passed?
GOLDEN: Columbus Johnson died from pneumonia. He sang and played at private parties. Somehow or another, he contacted pneumonia. At that period, pneumonia was a death knell, after a certain period of time. Henry Frank was possibly four years my senior, and Inus was maybe two years older than I. Inus and Frank--we called him Frank--Inus and Frank and I played together, grew up together. This was the household that I was in, up until the time that my parents moved to an area called Over the River. As I may be repeating myself, that was the most warmest, wonderful period of my life. I cherish that period of time till today. Many, many interesting things happened during that period of time. Many things.
DUNHAM:How old were you when you moved?
GOLDEN: I was five.
DUNHAM:Wow, and you remember--
GOLDEN: I remember.
DUNHAM:--many of those.
GOLDEN: Most of what I share with you will be, as I go back, as I go back recalling my history, a lot of it is brought on by music that I've heard. I'll share with you what happened at an age of maybe five, four or five. The house that my grandparents lived in, that I lived in with them, was up on the hill. A sketch of the house on the hill here. The big house on the hill, we called it. Every holiday--Christmas, New Years, special holidays--my grandfather would turn on his gramophone, the one you crank up, with the dog listening to his master's voice. I recall one special occasion. [phone rings] I thought I'd turned that stupid phone off.
DUNHAM:Do you want to pause?
GOLDEN: Yes. [interview interruption]
DUNHAM:So you were just telling a story about five years old.
GOLDEN: Yeah, five years of age. A lot of music. A lot of memories are brought on by music that I have heard, during certain periods of my life. This particular period of time, the memories that I recall were brought on, this particular one--. As I was saying, on special occasions like New Years and Christmas, they would open up the parlor, which was closed most of the year. My grandfather would turn on his gramophone, the one with the dog listening to his master's voice. I heard this song coming from out of that gramophone. It was Bessie Smith singing, [he sings] It rained for [sic] days, and the skies turned dark as night. This brings back a memory to me, as other songs have brought back memories of my life. That was one. But then after that, I moved away from my grandfather's house, my grandparents' home. My father and mother moved into an area we called Over the River. Over the River was a ghetto and had once been a red light district. There's a river that runs between uptown Athens--. Athens is a hilly place. If I can share with you, the story that I was told was that Athens came about because there were well-off people in Atlanta who wanted a school, a place for their children to study without distractions. A copy of Athens map on this page. So they created a private school, about seventy-two miles northeast of Atlanta. That school became the University of Georgia. Around the University of Georgia, Athens developed. Because Athens did develop around the university of Georgia, that was possibly less--how do I say?--less unbearable than other surrounding counties, where apartheid existed. I grew up in a town that I could not wait to get away from. I knew I was unhappy where I was, up until the age of nineteen; but just knew I wasn't happy where I was. Where I wanted to go, I had no idea.
DUNHAM:When you say Athens was less severe, it was still a segregated town. So what would be--?
GOLDEN: A good question. It's quite near Athens--the small county is outside of Athens, I think--where there was a lynching. So these small counties outside of Athens still had that Southern Klan-type existence. As I said, there was a lynching within a very few miles of Athens, during the time I was there. I have to share this with you, though. I could not wait to get away from Athens; but there was some people who saved me. Of all the negative that I have spoken of I want to share this with you. Hate is a heavy burden to bear. Hate is a terrible thing. If it had not been for three people in my life, during this period of time, I would've left Athens with hate. This, I don't have. I don't have hate for people. One of the people that helped me during this period of time was a woman that I worked for as a houseboy. I suppose middleclass and high class whites had houseboys. I worked for a lady named Penson. Her last name was Penson. Miss Penson's husband was a policeman. But Miss Penson did something for me that I treasure till today. She did not take away my dignity as a black boy. I worked for her when I was about eleven years of age. But she did not take away my dignity. She gave me something that I treasure till today. Her youngest brother and I played together, up until he reached the age of twelve; and then that friendship was severed, cut. The other person that helped me--.
DUNHAM:May I ask about that? When you say the friendship was severed, what was that transition?
GOLDEN: There was a system in that part of the country that let us black kids and white kids play together until--. I think, there was age, and I want to say the age of twelve. Somewhere around twelve or thirteen. At that time, this young man was told by his parents he could no longer be my friend. His parents were educators. His sister was, I say, the person who helped me to leave Athens without hate, because of her relationship with me. There was another person. I don't know if Miss Penson was Jewish or what. The last name Penson I've often wondered, is that a Jewish name? Her maiden name was Kirk. Her parents, I got to know. They were educators. But I don't know if Kirk is a Jewish name or not. [The] Jews played a major role in my development, during that period of time, and these things I will share with you as we goThe next person that played a major part in my leaving Athens without hate was a man by the name of Collins. I worked for him, as a delivery boy. This was after I left Miss Penson's employment. I entered high school. Then during the time that I entered high school, I went to work--. I dropped out. That's another story. When I dropped out of high school, I went to work at what is called Broadway Market. Broadway Market is on Broad Street, and about a half block from the gateway to the University of Georgia. I was his helper. He was a driver, and we delivered meat to all the small counties outside of Athens. Within, I'd say, about sixty miles or so surrounding Athens, we delivered. But I think ours was in Northeast Georgia. His name was Collins. Each morning when we were ready to make our deliveries, there was a make-up room. There were two drivers. Mr. Collings was the driver that I worked for; and there was another man, the other driver, whose name escapes me right now. And he had another helper that worked with him. A black man, who was older than I. But this morning, this particular morning, the story I want to share with you was that as we were waiting to get our meats and everything ready for delivery, he came up to me and said, "Hey, little nigger, go into the smoke room and get me some smoked links." I let out a little screech. In that make-up room, there was a butcher block, with some knives in it. I pulled out one of the knives, and I threw it at him. I missed him just by a hair's breadth. This man here was about six-two, weighed about 175 pounds. I was five-eight, and weighed about 143 pounds. He started toward me, and he would've picked me up and threw me across the room; but Mr. Collins stepped in between him [and me] and said, "No, don't touch him. He doesn't work for you." This made a marked impression on me, as a very young kid, that one white man would step in between another white man, on behalf of a black boy. Second person that allowed me to leave Athens without hate. This was the second.
DUNHAM:Had you experienced--sorry--being called that much in your upbringing?
GOLDEN: Nobody called me a nigger, under any circumstances. We didn't use that among each other. My parents, somehow or another, had a certain amount of class. Certain things we did and didn't do. We didn't use the term, that, with each other. No. No.
DUNHAM: Could I also ask, backing up just for a minute, how you got each of the jobs you got, versus working in the home?
GOLDEN: Possibly referrals; I'm not quite sure anymore. I'm not sure what happened. Could've been referrals from some member of my family or something. But that job, I'm not sure. Once we moved over the river, one of the people who was the brother of my aunt's husband--. My aunt, her husband and my mother and my father moved into that area Over the River. That's a story that maybe I should share with you, to give an idea of what was happening during that period of time.
DUNHAM:Yeah, I was curious, because you were so happy--
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:--and I assume your family was. So please.
GOLDEN: Okay. My mother and my father--and as I said before, he was a truck driver, and my mother worked in town--they and my aunt, my Aunt Magnolia who we called Totsie--. Aunt Totsie and Elvin, Elvin was my aunt's husband. He worked in a lumberyard, delivering lumber--. Yeah, he worked in a lumberyard. They decided, these two couples decided to buy a home "over the river." The river, when we say over the river, is what we call the Oconee River. The Oconee River is about four or five blocks from the center of town, and these houses parallel the river. [Narrator note: see enclosed map of Athens.] There was 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th Street, all dead-ended into River Street, which paralleled Oconee River. Along that street, that road, were the houses of prostitution. That started at 1st Street. As you left the center of town going west--. Or east. Going east on Broad Street, the center of town was at the gateway of the University of Georgia. University of Georgia was on the entryway of College Avenue and Broad Street. As you went east on Broad Street, about four blocks, was the river. River Street started at 1st Street, and paralleled the river from 1st Street, 2nd Street, 3rd Street, 4th Street. Further--East Athens? East Athens? Yes. Further east, maybe a block or so, were these houses of prostitution.
DUNHAM:These were white houses of prostitution?
GOLDEN: Yes, yes.
DUNHAM:Or some of both?
GOLDEN: Yes, yes, yes.
DUNHAM:In an otherwise black community?
GOLDEN: Well, yes. The answer to that, the short answer, is yes. You see, it seemed that there was a game playing. They were looked on in a strange sort of way.
DUNHAM:How do you mean?
GOLDEN: The University of Georgia, somehow or another, had a role to play in the creation of acceptance of prostitution, because it seemed to me--and this is my take--that they allowed prostitution for their young men, so that the young men would have some outlet for their sexual needs. So therefore, I think they halfway allowed this. Not so much as legal, but as a left-handed--.
DUNHAM:Looking the other way?
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:I know the University of Georgia was all white, correct?
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:But was it also all male?
GOLDEN: No.
DUNHAM:It was co-ed.
GOLDEN: It was co-ed. Yes, co-ed.
DUNHAM:But still, they felt the need for this outlet.
GOLDEN: It was co-ed, as far as I can remember.
DUNHAM:Well, with this white prostitution center there in the predominantly black community otherwise, were there ever tensions or run-ins relative to that?
GOLDEN: Wait, between who?
DUNHAM:Between white clients of the house--. I think of a house of prostitution and I think of potentially, alcohol and potentially other things, that could lead to fights or conflicts, in what was also a racially tense climate.
GOLDEN: Not between blacks and whites. Not between blacks and whites, there was not. First of all, there may have been, to some extent--and I'll share with you sometime before we finish talking--some incidents that happened during that period of time. This was a strange period of time, very strange. But let's look at it this way. When I was attending school, if I was walking home from school, going east down Broad Street--going west, east, west--going to my house, even during the time I was in school and prior to when I quit school for a while, if I was on the sidewalk and two or more white people--. I could not look a white woman in the face; let's not even talk about that. A white woman, I had to look off on the side, never could look her direct in the face. But if there were two or more white guys on the road, I'd have to step off the sidewalk, out in the street. If there was a single one, there would be no problem. A cowardly type of mentality. My father was what they call--. I don't know. My father was a bad dude. He did not create trouble, he did not cause trouble; but he did not run away from it, because he could handle himself.
DUNHAM:Well, and I've read of you talking about that in previous interviews and encounters, and I was curious, what I did not read of--. And he was a very big man, too, 6-4 or 6-5?
GOLDEN: About 6-6. He was 6-6 or 6-7, somewhere around there.
DUNHAM:What I did not read of--and I may have missed that--was if he had encounters with whites, where he--.
GOLDEN: Policemen.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Okay.
GOLDEN: I recall--good thing you brought this up--my father always worked. He was a hard worker. He was a truck driver for a coal company. But he also bootlegged. He bootlegged, and this was against the law. I recall one night--this was before he--. He and my mother separated. But I was asleep in the front room, and he and my mother were always friends. They remained friends forever. But I recall waking up, hearing these voices. I was asleep, and as I looked to where the voices came from, it was my father and my mother, and I saw his head was all bloody, from having been beaten with a blackjack. What he said was that--. There was a trestle on each side of Athens that crossed the river. Train trestle that crossed the river. This trestle was at what they called Madison Avenue. He had been caught on Madison Avenue, trestle running away from two of the baddest cops in town, who were known to get a black dude, Nelems Woods. And he ran to into Nelems Woods, and they beat him with a blackjack, until his head was like hamburger. But he said to my mother, "I cut one of them." So he cut one of them. But he got away. Next time I saw my father, he was on a chain gang in stripes, a chain gang uniform. Prior to that, I had seen my dad--. [he cries] Oh, man. Some of these memories, I want to forget. Some of them. Anyway.
They moved into this area, this housing area there. Right below me, in the house that my father and mother, along with my uncle and her aunt, was one of the houses. The madam there was Miss Mary I must've been eight or nine. I was Miss Mary's errand boy. Not solicitor, but going to Madison Avenue and doing shopping and stuff like that. Miss Mary was a fine madam. She was a nice lady. She lived so close to us that I could--. Possibly twenty feet. Her house was about, riverside, twenty feet from the house that my uncle and aunt and my mother and father lived in. So that's how close we were to the areas of prostitution.
DUNHAM:Did you know, at eight or nine, what type of bus it was?
GOLDEN: I guess so. I don't know if it made a big difference to me. I knew it. I wasn't naïve. But somehow or another, it just never made--. I know that outside of the house, where they dumped the ashes, was just loaded with condoms. Believe, this is close. Some of the younger kids would take the condoms as balloons. Can you imagine? That was right below the house. This was what I grew up with, until the age of nineteen.
DUNHAM:So I know your father was a truck driver and bootlegging. Your mother, when you moved here, what was she doing then? She had the two young boys.
GOLDEN: When my father and she separated?
DUNHAM:Mm-hm.
GOLDEN: Okay. She had a rough time. I think my mother was twenty years of age, when I was born. Twenty years; I think she was twenty years old {inaudible}.
DUNHAM:Okay. And we're hitting the heart of the Depression, right?
GOLDEN: She had it hard, she had it rough, I think. Oh, she was working at a laundry uptown--if you want to call it uptown. As I've said before, Athens was very hilly. It seldom snowed in Athens, but there was always, if it's wintertime--. You had four seasons. The winters could be pretty severe. There would be ice on the roads after a rain. My mother had to go up and down these hills, going to and from work, and would leave her two children with my Aunt Totsie, who was married to my Uncle Elvin. Okay. So she really had rough way to go. Later, she got married again, and in the same house, to a Sam Evans. I think she had another child, between the marriage to my father and her second marriage--. She was the first among us to die. There was a story about her, that she fell into a tub of scalding water when she was a baby, and she never totally revived from that. This is my oldest sister. They would heat the water in a washtub. My mother had boiled that water in a kettle and poured it into the tub. Evidently, she turned her back; and while her back was turned, this baby fell into that tub, backwards into that tub of hot water. Scald[ed] her all over her back. She never really survived from that. This was part of the life that my mother had to go through, along with having to--. I remember she would put padding on her feet, on her shoes, where she would not slip when she was going up and down these slippery hills of Athens, to get to and from work. That's the life she was living for many, many years.
DUNHAM:Yeah. How did you make do? What were you eating during those years?
GOLDEN: Scratching. Scratching whatever I could scratch, rake up. Because times were hard for everybody. There were some that lived a little bit better than others. But being a single mom, there was time when there was no food in the house. We did the best we could. When she and my father were together, though, we lived quite well, even though we were over the river. We lived quite well. But once they separated, it was a tough time.
DUNHAM:You said they remained friendly; but your father was not financially supporting you at that time, I guess, then.
GOLDEN: No, he was not, in a sense. Yes and no. One of the things that I guess I'm fortunate for is that although he and my mother had separated--. I say they may have gotten a legal divorce; maybe they did, maybe they didn't. But we lived on 2nd Street, two houses from River Street. My dad lived on 3rd Street, with his second wife. My dad was always somehow--and this is good for me--my father's influence was always there for me, at times when I needed it most. I recall that I was one of those young kids who played with all the little girls around the neighborhood, and he, my dad-- There was a Miss Kate, who lived up on 2nd Street, four or five blocks above the river. She had Walt, Charlie, Harold. She had at least three sons. One of her sons was a very good friend of my dad. His name was Charlie, Charlie Thomas. My dad and Charlie Thomas were very good friends. They would drink white lightning and just laugh, laugh, laugh. Walt Thomas was a minister. Walt Thomas played a major role in my life later on. Walt Thomas was a minister; he had a store, that store up on 3rd Street. Harold Thomas was half white, and a lot of this happened during that part of the world down there. There was a lot of mixed, mulatto people in that part of town.
DUNHAM:Was it always known?
GOLDEN: Well, it was kept under the covers; you could tell. But it wasn't something--. Nowadays, you've got mixtures of black and white--black men, white women; white women, black men. Back in those days, it was white men and black women; there was no other combination.
DUNHAM:Where the white fathers playing roles in those children's lives?
GOLDEN: No. Not openly. Not openly. They were there, and I think we all knew it; we all knew they were half. What it was, is that Harold Thomas was almost as fair as you are. So we knew that there was some white father, some white blood there, even about fifty-fifty, right? But I was playing around with one of his nieces, and he told my dad, he said, "Tell Ernest he better leave my niece alone." My dad came to me and said, "Look, Ernest. You leave--" What's her name? Harriet? "You leave Harriet alone, because whatshisname has said--." Whatever her uncle's name.
DUNHAM:Walt?
GOLDEN: No. No, it wasn't, but it was the other one.
DUNHAM:Oh, okay.
GOLDEN: Charlie? This was Harold, I think. "Says he's going to do something to you, and if he touches you, I'll kill him." So I said, "Okay, Dad." The next time he saw me, he was walking across the street, from 2nd Street to 3rd Street. He saw me over there with Harriet. He said, "Come on. Come on. Go home." He took his strap and strapped the living daylights out of me. That's one of the few times he ever touched me. But he said, "I told you not to." I remember and just, eh, felt that I deserved it. I should've listened to him in the first place, yeah? But.
DUNHAM:One of the things you said in an early interview is that while you did get physical punishment, your father always talked to you first about it.
GOLDEN: He talked with me--I probably told you--with that strap, his razor strap. [makes noises] But he did. I recall two times of my father ever touching me. I was a fairly decent--. I wasn't a good kid; I just wasn't a bum. I need a little break.
DUNHAM:Okay. Yeah, please. [interview interruption] Okay, we're back. Well, I wanted to ask you. You mentioned the minister.
GOLDEN: Thomas.
DUNHAM:Yes. What role did religion play in your life, growing up?
GOLDEN: Okay. Major role, actually. At the age of eight, nine, my mother was quite religious. A Baptist. We lived on 2nd Street, and there was a Methodist church on 3rd Street. For Sunday school, sometimes I would go to the Methodist church. But my mother was Baptist. Four blocks from the river, going east, was the Baptist church. This was the church that I attended, at the age of maybe nine. The minister, Walt Thomas, was not the pastor of that church. But one or two things happened during this period of time. One, I had a major breakthrough, as far as religions are concerned. What you would do when you attended church in the mornings, on Sunday morning, you went to the mourners' bench. At the mourners' bench, you knelt down, and all of the sisters, I suppose--. The pastor preached, and then the sisters came. As you were kneeling down, they would say, "Just touch my hands and you'll be saved," and that sort of thing. At that age, somehow or another, it seemed to me was more on emotions than on values. I questioned it. One of the things that I questioned was the Trinity. I questioned the Holy Trinity, at that age. I was afraid. I was afraid of my thoughts. I should go back, though, first, and explain to you about this third person.
DUNHAM:Okay. Okay, go ahead; then we'll come back to that.
GOLDEN: The third person was a Jewish man. During my period of time when I had--. Let me see. I was attending school; I had gone back to school. I worked for a man called Saul Boley. Saul Boley had a boutique uptown, about three or four blocks from the center of town, in the entryway to University of Georgia. He had almost a father-son relationship with me. Strange, for that part of the world. At that time, I only knew white people; I didn't make that fine distinction between Jews being white and whites being white and all that sort of good stuff. They were all white. But he did, somehow or another, say to this black boy, shared with me, talked to me as a father. There was an incident that happened in Georgia during that period of time. That was that the president of the faculty of the University of Georgia had advocated the utilization of a building on the campus. Eugene Talmadge was the governor of Georgia at this time. This president of this University of Georgia had advocated utilizing a building on that campus, so that black students, who graduated either from Athens and the surrounding counties, could attend it without going out of town. Eugene Talmadge got word of this. He fired the president, I think, and several members of that faculty. The outcome of that was that the University of Georgia was taken off the accredited list for two years. I don't hear anymore about this. I have heard bit, if anything, about that. But it was a major issue in Athens.
DUNHAM:They were fired for attempting to begin integrating the school.
GOLDEN: Integrating. You bring up about integration.
DUNHAM:In the 1940s?
GOLDEN: Yes, during that period of time. Now, I saw the letter. Fast forward to Hawaii, twenty, thirty, forty years later. I saw the letter, on the letterhead, from Talmadge to a minister here, thanking him--this minister here, whom I happened to know and knew his daughter, and his daughter introduced me to my wife--thanking him for helping to stamp out--I'm paraphrasing--the possibility of integration in the state of Georgia. I saw the letter, with his stamp on it.
DUNHAM:How did you come to see that letter?
GOLDEN: A friend of mine here, who was a radical. We'll come to that later on; we'll tell you about him later. But I saw the darn letter. I knew the man. I know this man, who was here. He's dead now. Okay. Saul Boley, I went to him.
DUNHAM:Can I ask, just on the man who was here, who had received the letter, in your life that you him here, then, he was a tolerant--?
GOLDEN: I didn't know him then. I didn't know him in Athens, no.
DUNHAM:No, but here. When you knew him here, did he show evidence of racism, I guess, if you will?
GOLDEN: This was a black man.
DUNHAM:Oh, this was a black man who helped stop the integration. Oh, pardon me.
GOLDEN: Thank you.
DUNHAM:But nonetheless.
GOLDEN: Thank you. No, he was a black man. And the story behind him is a story within itself. It's a story we should come back to. Okay. Now remember, I said I didn't know that fine distinction between white Jews and white {inaudible}; all these were white. Catholics had a pretty rough way to go down South, during that period of time. Catholicism wasn't totally accepted. But a strange thing would happen. On Jewish holidays, the bloody town would be closed. Shut down. Jewish holidays, closed. So this particular time, I asked my Jewish Calabash father, "Why do you people allow this?" He said, "Jews do not participate in politics." This is what he said to me. Up until then, as I have said, I didn't make the fine distinction between other white people. But that was the relationship between Saul Boley and me. Because of that, this was the other person that somehow, because of his relationship with me and his--. Yeah, his relationship with me let me leave Athens without that burden of hate.
DUNHAM:Although he personally mentored you, he also made clear that he also wouldn't take a stand. Because you're saying when you asked him why he allowed it to happen, you're talking about not allowing integration and that type of thing, is that right?
GOLDEN: Well, he was saying is that however, whatever was happening at the University of Georgia, he was not going to become a part of it, because Jews did not get involved in politics, and this was a political thing. That if the University of Georgia was taken off the accredited list by the governor of Georgia, that's political. I make that statement. Yes, it's a political thing. So when I went to him and asked him, "Why do you allow this?," he said, "We don't participate in politics." That made a marked impression on me, at that age. But more than anything else, it's his relationship with me. Sharing with me [a] man-on-man type relationship. I credit him for somehow or another salvaging me and saving my life, because hate is a burden that I don't need. Hate, [I] don't want.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Can you share a little more about the positive side of that mentorship and what he taught you about?
GOLDEN: Don't know if there's that much more. He ran a boutique. I was in school. I had gone back to school, and I had found my niche in school, the one that I had lost. That's another part of another story that I might share with you before the day is over. But I had dropped out. I dropped out of school, and I was out for almost two years. That's when some of those things had happened. You asked me about my religious background, and some of the things happened during that period of time when I had dropped out of school. But I did return. It was through my relationship with him, at that time, that I went back to school. Thank God, I found my niche. Other than that, I would've gotten married and been somewhere down in, possibly, jail. Anyway, I don't know. But he did make a major, major change in my life. Now, where was I going with the other thing, as far as my religious background?
DUNHAM:I'm curious, too, as far as religion. We've talked already a little bit about, and I know we'll talk more about, the influence of music. You talked about the connection between memory and music. I assume there was music in the church, as well. Was that a significant--?
GOLDEN: Okay. I go back to that time that I questioned the Holy Trinity. For what it's worth, I am a weird person in my beliefs, as far as who we are and what we intend to do here and why we're here. That, I'll share with you before the day is over. But if you want to know, I am supposedly a Bahá'í. I am not a good Bahá'í. There are twelve principles of the Bahá'í faith, and I'm a strong believer in the first five, which I will probably talk about later. But now, I questioned the Holy Trinity, and was afraid of the thought. Walt Thomas, who was a minister, he did two or three things. He created a choir of youth for young people, mostly in their late teens and early twenties. As a church member, they did choir things inside his church, and he traveled throughout the counties and things like that. I was one of the youngest people in that choir. I was maybe nine, ten years of age. We were reading sheet music, at that period of time. I could take the sheet music and read it. Do, re, mi, fa, sol, la, ti, do, the whole thing. No music instrument, just verbal reading from sheet music. I could do this at that age. So I sang with his youth group. But also he created another group of four of us. My aunt--my mother's youngest sister, Mary Anne, who we call Sis--my cousin John, who was three years my junior, and my cousin--. God, what was my cousin's name? Curtis. There was Sis, John, Curtis, and me. And I was the lead singer. You had four-part harmony. Okay. You had lead, tenor, baritone, and bass, okay? I was the lead, and the lead set the pace for all this whole thing. This is my background, at the age of nine. We were broadcasting at WGAU on Friday nights. I think Friday night or Saturday night, we were broadcasting on the radio station, WGAU, out of Atlanta. I sang the lead, John sang tenor, and Curtis sang baritone. Beautiful voice. Natural. He would've been Michael Jackson of that day, that type of personality. Just natural voice. Then did I mention my aunt?
DUNHAM:Yeah.
GOLDEN: She sang bass. So we were broadcasting. Rev. Walt Thomas was our--what do you call it?--promoter. He had us on the radio.
DUNHAM:What songs are you singing, at this time?
GOLDEN: [he sings] There are twelve gates to the city, foursquare wide. Hallelujah. Great God Almighty, won't you look over yonder? What a beautiful city. Well, hold me, oh, Lord. Truly, ain't you glad that it's a beautiful city? There're twelve gates to the city, foursquare wide. Hallelujah. That's what we were doing at that age.
DUNHAM:Very nice.
GOLDEN: Anyway. Now, we did that and we were broadcasting. That's just one; we were doing several.
DUNHAM:Yeah. You're going down to Atlanta at this time? How far is that?
GOLDEN: No.
DUNHAM:Are you recording in Atlanta, or you have a recording in Athens?
GOLDEN: We were doing all this is Athens.
DUNHAM:Okay, okay.
GOLDEN: I haven't gotten to Atlanta yet.
DUNHAM:Okay. But through the Atlanta radio station.
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. That station was in Athens. I don't know. It was a local station we were broadcasting out of. Anyway, as we were going home one night after a music engagement --. Remember, we'd go out to these various counties, church conventions, and we were the featured group, because we were the youth group, and we were a novelty. This particular night--. It might've been early evening. It was evening, because John and Curtis were in the back, backseat. I was in the backseat, too. He and Sis were in the front seat, and he propositioned my aunt. You can imagine, at the age of eleven, the sort of impression that left. He was a minister. I knew what he was doing, because my dad had already given me a strapping, for what he was doing. My dad had already given me that beating. But he was hitting on Sis. So I wasn't naïve. That somehow made me question religious leaders. After that, I guess things changed and took a difference, somehow. Anyway, many, many years later, that thought that I was afraid of as far as the Holy Trinity was concerned, I came to find out that I was not alone in that thought that I was afraid of. I was at a Bahá'í fire side, way, way, way, way later, and someone else said, "Look, Jesus was not God," and that was my redemption. Because as I said, I was afraid of that thought. But I was here. I had been in Hawaii for maybe thirty, forty years, when this breakthrough came to me.
Now, let's go to my next--. I went back to school. What had happened is that from the time that I entered elementary school through the fifth grade, I was always in the top five of the class. I remember when I was in second grade, that the teacher in the fourth grade would call me in to let me read for some of the slow students in her class, to show them what a second grade student could do. This will give an indication of the type of student that I was. We didn't have kindergarten; we started first grade. Mrs. Deadinyler, this tough, tough teacher, gave you the basics. Once you finished Miss Deadinyler's class, you were ready. So from first grade through fifth grade, I was in East Athens School. Always in the top five. Then I went to the Reese Street School, which was uptown Athens. I still maintained that top of the class position in the sixth grade. Reese Street School--. It had only sixth and seventh. That's what? Intermediate, I guess.
DUNHAM:Middle, intermediate, yeah.
GOLDEN: Okay. Okay. Then the teacher in the sixth grade was tough. I still maintained my top five position. Went to seventh grade, and the teacher there was a very friendly, warm teacher, but she never called on me to do anything. So for a whole school year, I wasn't called on to do anything. Miss Freeman, I think, felt that I knew everything. I don't know why, never knew. So long story short, when I went from seventh-- to Athens High, an industrial school. You graduated eleventh grade. It wasn't twelfth. Eleventh, okay? So when I reached the eighth grade, because I had done nothing in the seventh grade, I wasn't ready. When I reached there, and I could not keep up with what I had been maintaining up until that time, I became disenchanted and quit. This is when I went to work for somebody; I've forgotten who. Maybe that's when I went to work at Broadway Market and had these other incidents I shared with you. Okay. So I dropped out, and then I went back. A friend of mine--whose name I cannot remember--somehow or another encouraged me to go back to school. I was still living over the river. My mother had moved around, several different places, as a single mom. We used to live with my aunt, because this is where I grew up. So whatever I did, wherever my mother moved to, I'd always go back to Aunt Totsy's house. So where am I going? I'm going somewhere with this story. Anyway, when I got back to school, I somehow was encouraged by a friend, who encouraged me to go back to school, and I reentered high school. You see, I was one year older than those students, when I first went to high school. In the eighth grade, I was one year older. This time, I was--yes--I was with a group that I could relate to. Somehow I was back in that niche that I had missed. I was back where I wanted to be, with students that I wanted to be [with]. There was a glee club. For the first two years I was in the glee club, as part of the course. Then the second two years, I became one of the lead singers in the glee club. I was the lead singer. You had soprano, alto, tenor, and bass. But we did music set to lyrics. It was light opera. I starred two years in that. One of the songs that I do now, I found during that period of time.
Begin Audiofile 2 07-24-2014.mp3
DUNHAM:We're on. This is tape two, July 24, 2014, with Ernest Golden. You were just telling us about becoming a lead in the glee club in high school.
GOLDEN: Yes. I then found that position, that niche that I'd been accustomed to. I was back, had my stride back, making friends. Some of the greatest people that I have known through my lifetime was created during that period. [audiofile skips] --closest buddy, friend of mine, was Milton, Milton Jordan. Milton and I came to Hawaii together. We went through high school together, we traveled. Well, first of all, the first thing you did when you graduated was get the heck out of Athens.
DUNHAM:I'm about ready for us to get out of Athens, but I wanted to ask a couple other questions about your childhood, and then let's hear what you and Milton--. Your journeys, before you came to Hawaii. Back to your schools. Your school was segregated. In an earlier interview, you mentioned there were two black schools, is that correct? Was there a private school and a public school?
GOLDEN: Did I mention two? Where did you get that? Because there were.
DUNHAM:I thought in an earlier interview, you said--yeah--there was--.
GOLDEN: There were two schools. There was a private black school and there was Athens High and Industrial School. I've forgotten the name of a private school; however, a friend of mine--he and I became good friends--attended that school. It'll come to me.
DUNHAM:Yeah. I was curious. So in the private black school, what were sort of the occupations of the families, maybe, who went there, and who were a little more affluent, I guess, to be able to go there? Is that the case, do you have a sense?
GOLDEN: The school that I attended?
DUNHAM:Or the private school was the other one, too.
GOLDEN: I don't know too much about that school. As I said, one of the guys who did attend became a friend of mine, Edward, Edward Pope. His mother was a teacher, and I think she was a teacher at this--. The school name won't come to me right now. It was not too far from Athens High and Industrial School. Now, the name itself Athens High and Industrial School will indicate something, as to what was the theme of the school. We were being trained for what's industrial. We weren't being trained for professional--doctors and that sort of thing; we were being trained to be able to do building, do carpentry, and that sort of thing. Preparing us, where we'd be able to get jobs.
DUNHAM:More of a vocational--?
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:When you were young though, did you have particular dreams of what you wanted to be when you grew up?
GOLDEN: I recall that--. Who asked me? I think Miss Lucy Pinson asked me, "Ernest, what do you want to be?" I had been doing some drawings. I did some drawings at an early age. I remember I had a jacket, and I drew a eagle on that jacket. Me, as a black boy, [knew] nothing about art or being an artist. But the highest avocation would be a professor. So I said to Miss Lucy, "I want to be a professor." Later, I realized that I wanted to be an artist. But that came way, way later. But when she asked me the same thing you asked me, I said--. I wanted to be a professor.
DUNHAM:What did that mean to you, do you think?
GOLDEN: Educator. It was educator. In other words, head of a school, I guess, or teacher of a school.
DUNHAM:Your teachers and everyone who ran the school had been all black, at your school, correct?
GOLDEN: Yes. Oh, yeah, always.
DUNHAM:I was interested. I know you talked a little bit before, and Candice and I talked beforehand. But also you had quite a bit of black history in your school, was that correct?
GOLDEN: The year before I enrolled at Athens High and Industrial School, I think the year before I entered that, they were teaching Latin. But when I entered, the major subject was black history. I know I studied black history; but whether it was titled black history or whatever it was, but that was one of the major subjects. So I did have a pretty firm knowledge of that.
DUNHAM:Do you remember much of what it covered then, and how it covered it at all?
GOLDEN: I'd say I don't recall too much. However, I was a reader. As I told you, during the time that I was in the second grade, they called me to read before the students in the fourth grade. I read everything that I could get my hands on; consequently--. I think I'm going somewhere with this thought.
DUNHAM:Yeah. With the black history? Some books, some authors, maybe, you read?
GOLDEN: What happened was, now, English, yes, that okay, because you read in English. Math, eh, so-so. I think I possibly kept a B average. History, I was A number one. Whatever required reading, I seemed to excel. To the extent--and this is something that we might be interested in me talking about--is that Professor Duval, who was very conservative, who was the head of the school--. That school, at the time that we're talking [about], that period, may have had less than 400 students. The principal was Duval, Professor Duval. He taught history. Here's something we need to sort of make a point of. Duval took his top seven students--I was one, Milton was one, and there were about four or five others--on orientation to Tuskegee. I say that if it had not been for the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor, I would've gone to Tuskegee. Had I gone to Tuskegee, I would've gone into the military, because that was my leaning. The military was so orderly and everything; I always liked the uniform, the orderliness, the whole thing. So I'm saying if there had not been a bombing of Pearl Harbor, I would've gone to Tuskegee. Beautiful campus. One of the most beautiful campuses I'd seen. Never seen a black campus like that. I think I saw Dr. Carver in his laboratory, from a distance. Beautiful campus. Very impressed with it. I would've gone there. Now, somehow, we came back from that trip of orientation, and we graduated, this may have been the early part of May. I graduated in May, somewhere around May or June.
DUNHAM:Which year?
GOLDEN: June, 1943.
DUNHAM:June of '43?
GOLDEN: I graduated in June of '43. So much has happened from the time that I arrived here in Hawaii.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Well, since you were in high school after Pearl Harbor and all, can you tell me your memories of Pearl Harbor then, when you first--.
GOLDEN: Oh, when I first heard of Pearl Harbor? Interesting. I remember it was on a Sunday morning, and I think it was about nine o'clock. Somehow or another, President [Franklin D.] Roosevelt--I'm on the East Coast--said the hand that held the dagger stabbed into the back of his neighbor. You check it out; that was a quote that he made. The hand that held the dagger stabbed into the back of his neighbor. What had happened was that there were some negotiations down in Washington at that time. You see, we had placed an embargo on Japan. Or somehow or another, whatever the embargo was. But me, on the East Coast, Southeast, it was meaningless. I knew nothing about Pearl Harbor. Nothing at all. Sometimes I tell others who I talk with locally, that the only thing I knew about Hawaii was from Dorothy Lamour movies. Dorothy Lamour and Ray Milland swimming in the pools and that sort of thing. I knew nothing about Pearl Harbor, and didn't know that Pearl Harbor was in Hawaii. I say sometimes, jokingly, had I known Pearl Harbor was in Hawaii, I probably would've swam over.
DUNHAM:Well, you didn't know where Pearl Harbor was, but then the US joins the war. Is it having any impact on your community?
GOLDEN: None. None whatsoever. Not at that time. At that time, when I first heard of that attack on Pearl Harbor, it meant nothing to me. It seemed that later, though--and I'm trying to figure out how much later--it was a means whereby I was able to get the heck--. It gave me, and I'm almost certain others, the opportunity to get away from what I was running from. I was running from apartheid. The war gave me the opportunity to get out of Athens. So that is the number one tool that it gave me. It's a funny thing. I didn't know where I wanted to go. I didn't know what I would find when I got there. But I just knew I wasn't content where I was. Does that make any sense?
DUNHAM: Oh, yeah.
GOLDEN: I just knew I'm not happy where I am. So wherever I go, it's better than where I am. That was me in those days. So the war gave me that opportunity. It was meaningless, because it was so far away. I understand that at that time, the war was in Europe. The European war was going on. America had not gotten involved into that. It was only when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor that America become involved in the war.
DUNHAM:Did you know anyone who joined the military at that time, anyone who came West or elsewhere to do warfront defense industry work, in '42 or earlier in '43, before you graduated?
GOLDEN: No. No. I don't recall knowing or meeting anyone during that period of time. I know this much; that as soon as we graduated, Milton and I, the first thing we did is we would leave Athens. Once we started leaving, our first trip out of Athens was to New Orleans, Louisiana. Why New Orleans, Louisiana, I have no idea. But that was the first trip--and that was Milton and about five of us--[we] left Athens, going to, I guess, New Orleans. We got to Atlanta; my dad, at that time, lived in Atlanta. So when the five of us went to Atlanta--with little or no money--my dad hosted us for breakfast, all of us. After breakfast, we decided, hey, listen, we are short of money. So three of us, three of the group went back to Athens to get some more money. From where, I have no idea. So in the meantime, Milton and I waited for them to return, checked our bags to New Orleans, and went to a movie to kill time. They never returned. So now we had no money. We bought a box of Klix and some Kisses--those are those things wrapped in paper--and because we had checked our bags to New Orleans, we had to proceed to New Orleans, right? We couldn't go back; we had to go forward. Bags went forward, we couldn't go back. So we went to New Orleans with little or nothing, and went to work at a restaurant, had some breakfast and that sort of thing. The long story short is we stayed there two weeks. A lot happened during that period of time. Got to listen to some good blues and walk up and down Canal Street. Yeah, Canal Street. We worked in John Hotel. It was Jung Hotel we worked as bus boys. Because when you went to work in a restaurant as a bus boy, the first thing you did was eat. Okay? So we had that down to a fine finish the bus boy service. So we had our breakfast; after that, we could sort of stroll around the town, visit some blues houses. Blues all over, plenty of places. Small blues houses here and there.
DUNHAM:Did you know anybody there, before you got there?
GOLDEN: No. Yes and no. Now, there was a school there, and we had a friend in that school--maybe a girlfriend or something--and I forget the name of that school in New Orleans. But it was a first-class school.
DUNHAM:Tulane?
GOLDEN: No. No, Tulane was a white school. This, I think, was a black school. Very sophisticated school. It'll come to me, maybe, before our session is over. Anyway.
DUNHAM:Did you have, at this time, ambitions of a singing career or pursuing singing?
GOLDEN: What was my ambition in those days?
DUNHAM:But you had been a very accomplished singer and had some experience on the radio and professionally.
GOLDEN: Yeah. Yes, yes, yes, because remember, at this time, I had left high school with that lead position in the glee club. We had done two or three operettas, we called them, as lead. So I left high school with a pretty good background of music.
DUNHAM:Right. Did people ever busk, like they today? Was that ever a consideration?
GOLDEN: Do what?
DUNHAM:Sing on the street, that kind of thing. Did that happen then?
GOLDEN: Not around Athens.
DUNHAM:I just wonder, when it's hard to make ends meet, if that was ever--.
GOLDEN: No.
DUNHAM:Or in New Orleans?
GOLDEN: No, no. If they did, I'm not aware of it.
DUNHAM:But you made fast work as a busboy, and that helped with meals.
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM:So you were just in New Orleans for two weeks then?
GOLDEN: Two weeks. Two weeks. Then for some reason, we decided to leave; I don't know why. But evidently, it didn't serve our purpose, so we left.
DUNHAM:Had you been any of those places?
DUNHAM:Okay. But was this your first time down to New Orleans and through that country?
GOLDEN: Anyway. So we left New Orleans. Now I'm on a roll. On my way back. On our way back, somehow we picked up a Times magazine. In that magazine, there was an advertisement that said, there are jobs in Bermuda, civil service jobs in Bermuda. You go to the post office, to apply. So we went to the post office in Atlanta. This time, we evidently got on a halfway decent bus. I don't know how much money we had, but enough to get back, right? So when we got to the post office to apply, the person said, "We don't have jobs in Bermuda anymore, but there are jobs in Pearl Harbor." Now, at that time when I was working for Saul Boley, I was making three dollars a week, part-time, okay? Three dollars a week. I would work afternoon, after school, and on Saturday. So what does it pay. Now, remember, three dollars a week, that was my pay. "Seventy-two cents an hour." Big bucks, right? How much does it pay? Seventy-two cents an hour. For seventy-two cents an hour, I said I would go to hell and back for seventy-two cents an hour. So Milton and I signed up to come to Pearl Harbor. Okay? We signed up for an eighteen-month contract. Now, we then left and went back to Athens. We'd always go back to Athens--and this, we'll find out during our conversation--regroup and start out again. We went back to Athens. This was in October of 1943. 1942. October, 1942.
DUNHAM:Oh, did you graduate in '42, maybe? Sorry, I was trying to check that.
GOLDEN: I graduated in 1942.
DUNHAM:Okay.
GOLDEN: I graduated in 1942, June of '42, so all this activity must've been during that period of time.
DUNHAM:That makes more sense, because--
GOLDEN: Yeah, it does.
DUNHAM:--probably by '43, there would've been, even in Athens, more war-related effects.
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah, yeah. 1942, so I graduated 1942, okay? June of 1942. So then we went to New Orleans. Then we came back from New Orleans and regrouped, and then went to Philadelphia. I don't know how long we stayed in Athens. However we arrived in Philadelphia in October of '42. Yeah.
DUNHAM:This is after you've applied for Pearl Harbor?
GOLDEN: Yes. We were waiting for some sort of response to our application. Okay, so then we went to Philadelphia, in October. We rented an apartment. I went to work at a restaurant, and Milton went to work at another restaurant. But I do recall this incident. This is my answer to anyone who says, was I avoiding the draft? Here are my answers. I have three of them, and I'll share with you. Number one, at that time, they had the Army Air Force. Have you heard of the Army--?
DUNHAM:Yeah, yeah, because the Air Force was not separate yet. Right, right, right.
GOLDEN: It's the Army Air Force. I think they had a recruiting office, and I want to say down on Market Street. I'm not sure if that's--.
DUNHAM:Okay. In Philly?
GOLDEN: Yeah. I went there to volunteer for the Army Air Force. Now, remember after graduating high school, it was my intent to enroll in Tuskegee. I have always been impressed with the military.
DUNHAM:Attracted you, yeah.
GOLDEN: So when I applied, I was nineteen years of age. The recruiting officer said, "You will be drafted in January, so why don't you wait? At which time you can select the branch of choice." In other words, I'm playing with a thought that I had with this man, what he was saying to me, no, you cannot join the Air Force, because first of all, you're black, and blacks are not going to get in my Air Force. So he said no. He used diplomacy.
DUNHAM:Yeah, he gave you the runaround. Yeah, I know, okay.
GOLDEN: Gave me the runaround. Thank you. So I walked out, cursing underneath my breath, saying all kinds of--. Whatever it is I was calling him, because I knew what he was doing. Okay, when I got back to the apartment, Milton said, "I have my application for Pearl Harbor." I had my contract to Pearl Harbor. Okay, yours must be in Athens. We took off that same day.
DUNHAM:Can I ask, too about the application for Pearl Harbor? Because touching on this Army Air Force application, did you know that as an African American, there was an opportunity for you for this? Was it targeted towards African Americans at all?
GOLDEN: What?
DUNHAM:That the Pearl Harbor, that you would be considered for that. Because there's still so much Jim Crow racism, of course, in certain--.
GOLDEN: No, no, no. I don't think I had a feeling that I would be rejected to go to Pearl Harbor. That would've surfaced when I applied. You applied for [an] eighteen-month contract. So I had no feelings whatsoever that I would not be accepted; I just didn't know how long it would take.
DUNHAM:I see. Okay. You felt confident, yeah.
GOLDEN: Okay. What period of time I'd have to wait--. Waiting for acceptance or rejection. What they did, they did a full investigation. The FBI investigated you. They went back to my hometown of Athens and interviewed some of my friends that knew me. So one of my friends who was related to my aunt says that, "Hey, the FBI has been here talking to me. Been talking to me about you." Okay, so they investigated you--. But I passed with flying colors, right? How am I doing?
DUNHAM:Good. Good.
GOLDEN: So Milton said, "Listen, let's go to Athens, because your application should be there." So we took off. Got back to Athens, and sure enough, my contract was there. What they did was that--and here's what's interesting--I had been--. I have to back up. Somehow, before we went to New Orleans, we went back to Athens, and it seems that we went to Atlantic City.
DUNHAM:Yeah, I know you had been. I know from your previous interviews, you did spend some time there, yeah.
GOLDEN: We went to Atlantic City.
DUNHAM:That was the first place, I think, you went. No?
GOLDEN: I'm not sure. That's a good question.
DUNHAM:In previous interviews, I think that was before New Orleans. But is that significant to talk about, the time there?
GOLDEN: I don't think it makes that much difference. However if it does, I'll try to make some corrections, because I'd almost forgotten that. We somehow, during that in and out of Athens and going elsewhere, I spent three months in Atlantic City. We arrived there Memorial Day. Went to work at Bingham's Hotel, on Memorial Day.
DUNHAM:So I think that was right after graduation, Memorial Day, probably.
GOLDEN: That may have been. So did we go to Atlantic City before Philadelphia?
DUNHAM:I think so, from your previous [interviews].
GOLDEN: Maybe. Maybe. Must have.
DUNHAM:That was also before you'd applied, perhaps.
GOLDEN: Yes, I think so. That may have been.
DUNHAM:But you went back this time to Athens, and you got your acceptance. So did you both have the same acceptance? Eighteen-month contract?
GOLDEN: Yeah, that's clear. But okay, bring that back to--. Once we do this, let's go to Atlantic City, okay?
DUNHAM:Okay, okay.
GOLDEN: Now, okay, when we got there and the two contracts, right? His and mine were both there. Mine was there. They had cleared me. The FBI and all that sort of stuff had cleared me. Somehow, I had spent time with my dad, during the interim. Because I know I took a picture with my dad. I had been to Atlantic City, because I had this coat. I had purchased a camel hair coat, and I took pictures with my dad when he was in Atlanta, he and I together, and that will show that coat, he and I together. That's important, because somewhere, when I got to Athens and had that eighteen-month contract, I pawned the darn coat. I pawned it or sold it, for traveling money. I remember leaving Athens, going to California with something like fifteen dollars. Now, Milton and I signed up at the same time, we were approved, somewhat at the same time, but the routes were different. There was the northern route, and the southern route. I had the southern route. You traveled by train, I went down to the train station in Athens; but I went through Texas, all down the southern route.
DUNHAM:Both leaving from Athens and going to the same place, but they gave you--?
GOLDEN: Yeah, but I went the southern route, he went the northern route.
DUNHAM:Interesting.
GOLDEN: We didn't get together until we got to California. He arrived in Vallejo, California before I did, because my train took me--. I don't know why--. Again, one of those slow deals. I remember going all the way down through Texas and the over that route down that way. Strange, very strange. So I was on the train. Somewhere in the Midwest, I'd gone to the restroom and I lost that little fifteen dollars or so that I had. I don't know when I lost my little money, but somehow or another. And I've got days to travel. I've got hours to travel, before I can get to California. I've got first-class accommodations, because you traveled first-class. I've got first-class accommodations, I'm sleeping in a berth. Hungry again. Starving, and no food. The conductor saw this little boy, this black dude, sitting over there without any food, and he was nice enough to share--share his lunch with me. He saw me. Didn't see me eating at all in the lunchroom. Because you traveled per diem, but you got paid once you reached your destination. The military thing. You didn't get paid right away, you had to sustain yourself.
DUNHAM:Get reimbursed.
GOLDEN: Yeah. Yes, get reimbursed. So I arrived in Vallejo. Mare Island. Mare Island. Milton was there when I got there, so we hooked up again. But he had gotten there before me. Things sort of stabilized again for a while. I don't know how long we remained at Mare Island, before we went to San Francisco.
DUNHAM:You stayed at Mare Island in the barracks there? Do you recall?
GOLDEN: We weren't there that long. We weren't there that long.
DUNHAM:You weren't there that long, okay. So you don't have an impression about--?
GOLDEN: No, I have no impression of--. But I do recall staying there long enough to somehow or another enjoy some of the entertainment there.
DUNHAM:Well, can you describe that? I ask because Mare Island is right by Port Chicago, which maybe we haven't talked at all yet about, but where there was later the explosion, in '44.
GOLDEN: I don't know enough. Frankly, I don't know enough. I do know this much, that we were there long enough for me to have taken in some of the entertainment.
DUNHAM:It was in Vallejo, maybe?
GOLDEN: I think it was somewhere around Vallejo, because we were calling it Valley-Joe.
DUNHAM:Bars or clubs? Okay, okay.
GOLDEN: Yeah, somewhere in there. We called it Valley-Joe. Somewhere, because there was a singer. If I can remember what he was singing, it will give you an idea of who he was and where we were. The song was "I Wonder" by Lenna Johnson.
DUNHAM:That memory-music combination.
GOLDEN: Yeah. I wish I could remember. The guy was awesome.
DUNHAM:Well, if it comes to you, feel free to step back and share it.
GOLDEN: I will share it with you. I'll bring it back, if it does come to me, because you'll be able to pinpoint where we were, and almost how long we were there. But from there--.
DUNHAM:Were you with a group of all black men, do you recall, or mixed?
GOLDEN: Don't recall. I don't recall too much about that phase of it, the period of time we were there. I just know that something had stabilized and we were in the process of finishing up what we had set out to do.
DUNHAM:You weren't getting any training there or something like that, though?
GOLDEN: No. I'm trying to see if we went to work at all. If we did, then we would've stayed longer than I thought.
DUNHAM:So it sounds like a brief time in Valley-Joe or Vallejo, and then you headed up to San Francisco? Is that where you shipped out of?
GOLDEN: Yeah. Yes, we went to San Francisco.
DUNHAM:Did you get to spend any time there?
GOLDEN: Yes, we did. I do remember. The Kirkland Hotel? But I recall that where we were in San Francisco, the Japanese, American Japanese, had lived there.
DUNHAM:The Fillmore?
GOLDEN: This very, very beautiful area of well-kept homes and shops and things like that, that had once been owned by the Japanese, because the Japanese had been put in internment camps. Okay? They were just taken out, but it was well-ordered. We stayed at this hotel, waiting for transport to come to Hawaii. How long we stayed there, I've forgotten. It might come to me before our session is over.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Did you go to music clubs in San Francisco?
GOLDEN: I'm sure we spent time doing things like that. I don't recall any specific club that we'd gone to.
DUNHAM:Do you remember any initial impressions of California? Did you have an idea of what California was like?
GOLDEN: The only one that sticks in my mind is that wherever we were was such an orderly place, that the Japanese had created. Maybe other things will come to mind. But the orderliness of it was the impression. We were waiting for a convoy assignment. This may have been in December. Yes, I think the latter part of December. Or January. Might be January by now, I think. May have been January.
DUNHAM:Yeah. So you're in the Fillmore District, probably.
GOLDEN: Fillmore District, yes. That was the Fillmore District. Yeah.
DUNHAM:Which is now primarily African American.
GOLDEN: Is it? But at that time, the Fillmore District--.
DUNHAM:Well, after the Japanese had been incarcerated.
GOLDEN: I don't know whether it was primarily African American. I think it was just sort of Americans now. Our so-called mixture, other than Japanese.
DUNHAM:Let me know if you need to take a break. We can break any time. Did you know anyone in California, besides your friend Milton you were with?
GOLDEN: No. No, no, not that part of the country. No.
DUNHAM:Do you remember meeting people out here? Or I mean there.
GOLDEN: No, no. Doesn't register.
DUNHAM:So what happened when your troop ship came in?
GOLDEN: I'm trying to see if it was Milton on the same ship with me. He must've been. We must've arrived here together. That's a part of my life that's sort of a blank.
DUNHAM:Oh. Well, no problem. Well, maybe we should take a short lunch break, before we arrive in Hawaii, if you will. Does that sound good?
GOLDEN: That's a good place.
DUNHAM:Okay. Yeah, let's do that. Let's pause.
Begin Audiofile 3 07-24-2014.mp3
DUNHAM:Okay. So it's July 24, 2014. We got a little nourishment; we're beginning tape three with Ernest Golden, and we've moved outside to enjoy this beautiful space. You purchased the land in 1949. But that's later, sorry. Right now, I think you're just about to get on a troop ship, to come from San Francisco to Hawaii?
GOLDEN: Yes. Okay.
DUNHAM:Tell us about that.
GOLDEN: Prior to my conversation with you, may I share some things with you?
DUNHAM:Certainly.
GOLDEN: First of all, I would like to say that what I share with you now may or may not be the thinking of several highly educated blacks in Hawaii. A lot of them don't agree with me, what I say, my approach to what we did, why we did it, and the outcome. So I would like to say that, first of all, because there were so many that don't agree with me, don't agree with what I'm going to say. I would like to say this, also. That most of what I have started out on is, to a great extent due to, I would like to give credit to Delores Guttman. She has done so much, as far as bringing out the history of blacks in Hawaii, that information that was available to her and others, prior to 1941. Prior to 1941, or the bombing of Pearl Harbor, there's certain information, as far as blacks in Hawaii--African Americans, if you want to use the term--that's available. A lot of historians have somehow or another taken advantage of that, and written several books. But that information was available in two main sources. One was the University of Hawaii, and secondly, the [Bernice Pauahi] Bishop Museum. The information I want to share with you is possibly known by so very few who are alive today. That is the period between 1943, when I came here, and maybe one or two who came one year before I did. One of the people who came here one year before I did, that was here and that is still alive, is Bertha Dunson. So please, by all means, if you want to pursue and get some of the information that I'm sharing with you, and if you want to question anything that I share with you, Bertha Dunson might be able to put some light on it. But I want to give you information that's never been shared before. First of all, this period of time between the time that I came here, 1943--. I arrived here in 1943, from a small town in Athens, Georgia. I was running away from apartheid, and this gave me the opportunity. The bombing of Pearl Harbor and the eighteen-month contract gave me the opportunity to get away from that which I was running away from. I came from the South. There were 400 of us, on a troop transport that consisted of 6,000 military [and] civilian people. I want to call it the Tyler, but don't quote me on that. I think that was the name of the ship. On that troop ship of 6,000, there were 400 of us. I was from the South. And if others of us were true to their origin, I would say that they also were from the South, direct or to some extent indirect. We were waiting in San Francisco, in the early part of January. Of January, 1943, we were in San Francisco, waiting for a convoy to take us to the territory of Pearl Harbor. We waited for three days on the ship, at the docks, waiting for this convoy. After we were assigned a convoy, the voyage then started. Because at that time, the Japanese submarines had total control of the Pacific Ocean. They controlled it. So therefore, we joined a convoy to accompany us, or to guide us through. We were with this convoy for nine days, zig-zagging across the Pacific. For three days of that nine days, I was seasick. For three days, I was seasick. Eventually, I got over my seasickness. We were on the high seas. The blue indigos of the Pacific Ocean were unattractive to me. They were sort of alien to me. But eventually, we came to the warm waters of the Pacific Ocean, and I was over my seasickness. I would come out to stand on the deck and watch, as we traveled through the Pacific Ocean to the territory, at that time, of Hawaii. I arrived here in Hawaii February 1, 1943. As I say, I was from the South, and there were 400 of us blacks on that ship. We were assigned to an area that was called Civilian Housing Area 3. That's an area where the main airport is now. There are things that I can't recall. As I go, hopefully, I will recall. But let me proceed.
DUNHAM:Okay. Can I ask, the 400 blacks, were they all civilians, or were some of them military?
GOLDEN: The 400 blacks that arrived with me were civilians. We were all civilians. As I say--and this is where it becomes debatable--is that I was from the South. There's a reason for me saying that, is that I say that most of us, that 400, were from the South. That 400, most of us were from the South, if we were true to ourselves, either direct or indirectly. Now, why is that pertinent? Because I say--and remember, this is my take; these are my thoughts--I say we were using, or we've used, the vast Pacific Ocean as those blacks who were escaping slavery of the South used the Underground Railway to get away from slavery. I say we used the Pacific Ocean as they used the Underground [Railroad]. We said once we are here, we put this vast Pacific Ocean behind us, and we decided that we would do everything possible to lose our identity. We deliberately assimilated into this community and put everything related to that we were running away from behind us. In doing so, we were successfully, and we intentionally lost our identity. This is where some blacks in Hawaii will disagree with me. But I say that it was a shortsightedness that it was a mistake to do this. For so long, I have been trying to rectify this wrong that we brought upon ourselves, because in doing so, we lost most if not all of the knowledge of our history. We lost political clout, and we lost any civic clout that we may have had. We lost three things. We lost identity, civic and political. As such, we are nomads, because of that. And it was done intentionally. Now, the present governor, in 1985, said this at a meeting that was held. At that time, I think he was city councilman. Said that, you people are invisible, at a meeting that was held at the Hale Koa Hotel. A man named Harold Franklin brought that meeting together on a Sunday. It was a prayer breakfast, and he brought together something like 105 professional and black businesspeople in this community. And Abercrombie, at that time, was a councilman, and he was the keynote speaker. At that time, he made the statement that, "you people are invisible, and that politicians count." If he was here now, I would bring this to his attention and remind him of his quote. But from that day forward, I use that to say that we need to rectify that wrong. Again, I say that all the blacks in this community don't agree with my way of thinking. The newcomers, those that have come more recently, the younger people, seem somehow more easily ready to grasp that concept. But some of us who've been here for a long period of time say, no, we don't need it. But I stand firm. I stand firm on what I say. I remain where I am, because I know that I'm right. We were housed in a segregated area, at Civilian Housing Area 3. Blacks were segregated to an area, 18th and 19th Street. It was a segregated area.
DUNHAM:Was that a surprise, to get here--you have the distance of the Pacific Ocean, this new horizon--to find some aspects of segregation and Jim Crow still there?
GOLDEN: At that period of time, segregation was the law of the land. So I didn't question it, you accepted it. You didn't expect it to be here in Hawaii. You thought maybe you were getting away from it. But we weren't, because we were traveling under naval orders. Therefore, I was not.
DUNHAM:So the housing was segregated. Some things were segregated and some things were not, is that correct?
GOLDEN: Some things were not. First of all, where the airport is now is where some of the facilities existed. I'm trying to go back in my memory and see which were segregated. There was a banquet hall, there was a beer garden, there was a mess hall, there were barbershops. But I know the barbershops were for blacks, and some for whites. I don't know if we shared the banquet hall together or not. The beer garden, we may have all went, shared the beer garden. The mess hall, I'm not sure.
DUNHAM:This is on military property or civilian?
GOLDEN: Oh, all military, with civilian occupants. We as civilians were in that area. It was called Civilian Housing Area 3. Short, for CHA 3, we called it. When I first arrived, we were given our housing quarters. One of the things that you might find interesting is that during that period of time, the transportation around the military complexes--Ford Island, Pearl Harbor proper--was a little trailer-type thing that was pulled by a tractor. Slow enough for you to get on and off, without it having to stop. It was called Leaping Lina. As you passed by, you leaped on; and when you got to your job site, you jumped off. I remember. But you always had to go through the main entrance of Pearl Harbor, the main gate. I recall the first time that I was going to my job, I went to the job assignment. As I entered the main gate of Pearl Harbor, I recall looking to my left, and saw the devastation, the ships that had been bombed. Some of them, upside down, there were pipes and there was oil, and there was everything that I didn't want to be involved with. At that point, if I'd had the opportunity, I think I would've turned around and gone back home. However, we kept on traveling, until we reached the ferry landing, which is where I had to go, in order to get to where my work assignment was. We went through some base, until we reached the ferry landing. We boarded the ferry and went to my job assignment, which was Ford Island, that naval air base.
DUNHAM:Can I ask for a second? Sorry. When you said you might've, at that moment, had the choice to turn back home, is that seeing the devastation in the face of war, or what exactly was that?
GOLDEN: It's just first of all, I don't like working in dirt and mud and getting my hands dirty. Frankly, if I had to work there, if I had a choice, I would've quit.
DUNHAM:Okay. Did some people have to work cleaning up there?
GOLDEN: Oh, yes, actually, some people worked there. The money was probably better. I'm sure it was better than what I made, because I was getting seventy-two cents an hour. I think if you worked Pearl Harbor proper, you were earning more than that per hour. But I wouldn't have traded for the dollars. Never.
DUNHAM:Yeah, yeah. Okay. So--sorry--back to what you were doing at Ford Island then.
GOLDEN: Okay. So I was assigned, once we reached the ferry landing, which is where the Arizona Memorial Center is now. We landed over there, and then I was assigned to--there's a term for it--the administration department of Ford Island. I was assigned there, along with several other men who had less or none special abilities or professions. So I worked with a crew of men who did labor work, out in and around Ford Island.
DUNHAM:Who else was on your crew?
GOLDEN: The people that I had an opportunity to work with, and came to appreciate, and even to care about, were all of the ethnic groups that existed at that time, with one exception. The Japanese, because of the bombing of Pearl Harbor, were not allowed on military bases. However, I worked with Filipinos, Koreans, Chinese. There was a Chinese kid, there were Hawaiian kids about the same age as I.
DUNHAM:Portuguese?
GOLDEN: Portuguese. I worked with Portuguese, Filipino, Puerto Rican, one or two Chinese kids.
DUNHAM:Were there Caucasians?
GOLDEN: Yeah, one. There was a haole kid with us, that worked with us, also.
DUNHAM:Okay. Were most of them from Hawaii?
GOLDEN: Yes, the Filipino were from the fields, the pineapple fields and the sugarcane fields of Hawaii. They had been brought here by the Big Five, to work the fields, the pineapple and sugarcane fields. The work at Pearl Harbor gave them an opportunity to get out of the fields, and we were working together. Others were just young people. I was nineteen, and the rest of those people I was working with was probably about the same age, with the exception of the Filipinos, who were older. Because they didn't speak the language very well, so therefore, they were relegated to the same type of crew that we were.
DUNHAM:Were there many other African Americans?
GOLDEN: There was one or two that I got to know. But most of those that I knew had a degree of experience or professions, to the extent that they were not field workers or labor workers, as I was.
DUNHAM:I see.
GOLDEN: But they were assigned to the same department. Eventually, I had the opportunity, before I finished my first eighteen-month contract, to move up. Eventually, I was assigned to the tool shop. This was distributing tools and that sort of thing. So my high school education somehow or another came to play, and I utilized that in order to try to get me a halfway decent assignment. I've never liked dirty work, getting my hands dirty, so whenever I could find a way to get away from soiling my hands, I did it, and this was an opportunity.
DUNHAM:You said you came over with 400 African American civilians. Where were most of them now?
GOLDEN: Then? At that time?
DUNHAM:Yeah. After you got here, where did they go?
