GOLDEN: It's hard, it's difficult to say, because somehow or another, once we reached here to Hawaii, some of those, I'm sure, had more professional experience than I did, and they may have gone to other departments of Pearl Harbor. A young man who later became a good friend was working in the administration building, doing office work. But I didn't know him until later on. So they were doing various jobs around Pearl Harbor and Ford Island and that sort of thing.
DUNHAM:Were there any women in your group?
GOLDEN: No, however, there were women in the office. Women, at that time, there were very, very few. One of the things that is notable, at that period of time--and you can quote me--I think there was a study that said there was 105 men to every woman in the Territory of Hawaii. 105 men to every woman. So women were a premium. They were something sought after, because they were rare, very rare. Very few times did we see them.
DUNHAM:That must've made dating very challenging.
GOLDEN: Oh, it did because if you recall, when I was in Athens High and Industrial School, there was something like forty-four students that graduated in 1942, June of 1942. There were forty-four students. Of the forty-four students, fourteen were boys. So we, the boys of the class of '42, had a field day. So you can imagine the cultural shock when I came to Hawaii and found out that there was 105 men to every woman. Some of my friends and I set out to somehow or another remedy that problem, if you want to call it that.
DUNHAM:How so? How did you approach that?
GOLDEN: Some of us got together. It's amazing what you can do if you're pushed into a corner. We created a social club, some friends and I. I think this may have been my second contract, though--. The second group of war workers that came to Hawaii was a different crew. They were more highly educated than the first group. I'd finished my first contract, gone to Athens, Georgia, stayed a month, came back for my second. So most of this information now comes from the second contract. There were two or three buddies of mine, and I started a school of what I call seduction. We made an art of pleasing women. We knew everything. We knew how to light a cigarette, we knew where to sit, we knew what time of evening to go out. About this time, the war had moved further into the Pacific, and downtown Honolulu had opened up, but in Honolulu there was a 10:00 p.m. crew eve, so that those of us, what we call sometimes now the in crowd, would go down to Smith and Hotel Streets, where all of the night people were. These were bartenders, barmaids, waitresses, call girls. What we would do is, we'd say that if you go out early in the evening--. And the place closed at ten o'clock. Some of the young-ol' guys would go out around six o'clock in the afternoon and sit down in the bar, trying to talk to a woman at the bar, a waitress. By nine or ten o'clock, she'd heard all their stories. So we would go out about nine o'clock in the evening--fresh, got plenty money--and then we would take over where they left off, but by this time--. But we made an art of it. Then eventually, we created what is called the Ten Bachelors. That was the second group. One of my buddies who worked in the administration building in the corps, he and I, along with--. This was still 18th and 19th Streets. Cork and I, we pretty much, the Ten Bachelors. But there were eight others of us; there were ten altogether. What the Ten Bachelors did was go around downtown to the various clubs that we visited and invite all of the women in the club to a private party. These parties were dress. But you'd come by invitation only. Therefore, we had an opportunity to have our choice of all these women who came to our parties, because our parties were first class. We'd hire a band. It was all invitational. So you didn't pay anything; all you had to do is be a friend. The women loved us, because they could dress up and come out and have a good evening at our parties. We had a band, there was a well known bandleader at that time. At the armory on the palace grounds, we gave a party.
DUNHAM:So did you rent that out?
GOLDEN: We would rent a hall. Every once in a while, we'd decorate that building. There was a banquet hall in the CHA 3 area. We would rent that hall for some of our private parties. Decorate it, hang up fliers and balloons and all that good stuff. Oh, yeah, you could dress formally, as formal as you wanted to be, because it was well done. There was good music. All you had to attend was be a friend of the Ten Bachelors.
DUNHAM:Okay. Were any other men allowed to come to these parties?
GOLDEN: Yes. We had buddies and friends. However, by this time--this is my second contract--women were allowed to join their husbands at CHA 3. We became so successful with the Ten Bachelors that the husbands created a club called the lords of the manor. This was somehow or another to offset the popularity of the Ten Bachelors, which was never really accomplished. They tried, but failed.
DUNHAM:So what is the ethnic makeup of the women? There's this scarcity of women.
GOLDEN: Local. Most local. However, by this time, there were some black women here. I dated for a while one of the first black women that was here when I arrived. I'm presently writing a story of her. Quite attractive. I think she's from somewhere in Ohio, but was here when I arrived in Hawaii. She was either married to or living with a Puerto Rican guy. They lived in that area where the present airport is, the Damon Tract Area that used to belong to Hawaiians. There was a Hawaiian area that someone--Luke, I think--bought it, bought the Hawaiian property. But there was housing in that area, right near where the main post office is now.
DUNHAM:So there's these various aspects of segregation and Jim Crow here in Hawaii; but were there any obstacles to interracial dating?
GOLDEN: Hawaii is--was, still is--different. The short story is segregation or prejudice in Hawaii is not organized. That doesn't mean that it doesn't exist; it's just not as it was in the South. In the South it was the law of the land, it was organized. Here--and this is one of the things that I like about Hawaii--I am free to choose my friends, regardless of ethnicity. I can choose my friends--white, black, brown, green, or what have you. This, to me, is important. But that's the difference. It does exist. I became very much aware of it when my wife and I were first married--. My wife would go out and try to find an apartment, because we realized, after we'd been married for about a year or so, that we needed to get out of the bachelor apartment, which we didn't have up until that time. I got married in 1950. Up until then, I lived with one of my ten bachelor friends. He and I had an apartment up on Miller Street, just about a block or so above the Queen's Hospital. We were renting from a Japanese man. I think the man's name--I don't mind mentioning his name. He was a nice landlord, Funikoshi. Funikoshi had some apartments up there, so he rented to blacks indiscriminately. One of the few places that blacks could find, people who would rent to them.
DUNHAM:So housing was an obstacle, finding housing?
GOLDEN: Housing was not something that you could just walk into and rent. To answer that question. We married in 1950. My wife is fair Portuguese. I was working at the airport--this was my mainstay employment, at this time, after I left Pearl Harbor--I would work twenty-four hours on and twenty-four hours off as sky caps. As a bachelor, my buddy and I had a bachelors pad. When he was working at the airport also, I was at home; when I was at home, he was at work. So I had the pad to myself. But when I got married, that meant that someone was there all the time, so the bachelors pad became non-bachelor. My wife became pregnant then and we had a child, and we had to move. tThe long story short is that she would go out, during the time I was at work, and look for an apartment. She'd go to these people, they'd say, yes, it's available. But I'm the one that's going to pay the bills and sign the contract. So when my black face showed up, suddenly it became rented. This happened more than once, and eventually, I realized what was happening. So I reached the point where I said, "Listen. I am--. " I think I may have used the term Negro; I don't think we were using blacks in those days. "I'm of Negro ancestry. If you have any reservations about renting to me," this is when I would call them on the phone, "let me know now, because I don't want you to waste my time nor I waste yours." This upfront approach caught the person off guard but not be able to come up with an answer. I tried this more than once. Eventually, I called a man and said, "Listen," and gave him the same spiel. He said to me, "I don't care what color you are, as long as you pay my rent." This man and I became friends. He was Kaimuki plumbing, at that time. His name will come to me, Ed Camfous, because he became a friend of my family. He's the one that did the roof, the plumbing for my house.
DUNHAM:Oh, okay. Before you found this man, did you think about challenging legally? Could you have? Or did you think about that type of approach?
GOLDEN: Didn't think about it. Didn't think about it. I just sort of, I guess, decided, hey, look, this is your life, the way of life in Hawaii, this is your place. But there were others who did. There were other black leaders who did, and these are some of the people that we may want to talk about. I need to give you some names, because there were civic leaders. But in sports and music, especially jazz. One of the well-known jazz musicians of the day is Trummy [James] Young. Everyone seemed to know about Trummy Young, but there were others. There was Ernie Washington, who was a piano player. No one seems to remember too much about Ernie Washington. I would like to bring knowledge of Ernie Washington's time here. Friend of mine, whom I have got to know. Let me see if I can think of others who may have played a major role. Que Martin also was a key player during that period of time. Que played music down at the Swing Club, on Hotel Street. He was one of the people who were instrumental in us going into Waikiki at this period of time. Waikiki didn't necessarily ban you because you were black, but they did not welcome you because you were black. So the only ones who really were welcomed into Waikiki were musicians--black musicians, black entertainers. Others of us did not get into, were not welcomed into Waikiki. For some reason, they didn't hold out the candle and say, come on.
DUNHAM:Well, I know you said it was informal, not like it was in the South; but that sounds pretty strong, that you knew you weren't welcome. How was that known to you?
GOLDEN: How was that known? In many, many ways, many subtle ways, that you knew that you weren't welcome there. That even exists still today.
DUNHAM:Oh, really?
GOLDEN: I walk around this neighborhood now, and it's not, hi, Mr. Golden or hi, Ernest. And I do business with you or I spend my money with you, and I've done this for several years, and you don't know my first name? You don't know my last name? You can't even mention my name, and I'm spending money with you? That happens here, daily, in this community in which I live. And I conduct myself in a manner so that no one should deny me or say that I'm not worthy of at least a decent greeting. So how they did this? It was so subtle, I don't recall. I don't recall. I know at the time that I came, for the first eighteen months, is that number one, the Royal Hawaiian, the Moana Hotel were all camouflaged. The Royal Hawaiian was under camouflage. The barbed wire was all around all the beaches, so the beaches were not accessible during this period of time. Between the Kalakaua Avenue and the Ala Wai Canal, were houses that were housing pretty much those people who worked at the hotels. Most of those, at that time, I would say, were Hawaiians. But eventually, they moved the Hawaiians out, somehow or another, and others started coming in and taking over.
DUNHAM:Well, talk a little bit about the militarization of the island, the martial law. You mentioned the barbed wire around the beaches. Can you describe that scene? Because that's something totally foreign to the rest of the Home Front experience.
GOLDEN: Okay, okay. Martial law existed. In other words, you had to be off the streets at ten o'clock. At Civilian Housing Area 3, where we lived, we'd be out, downtown Honolulu, at the clubs or wherever we could be. Let's come down to basics. As a young man, we were looking for female companionships. Anything, as far as women were concerned; we wanted, whatever way you want to call it, okay? So you could go downtown in what they called a J day. That was your day off. There were lines. Most of this activity was from River Street to, I think Nuuanu Street. If you're going up King Street, you start at River Street, and you go up Townside, toward Waikiki, to Bishop Street. Or Nuuanu Street. Nuuanu, Bishop. Nuuanu Street was pretty much the cutoff. From that part, going toward Waikiki was pretty much big business. That's where the power was. But from River Street up to Nuuanu Street, and from Beretania Street to Hotel Street, were red light. I was going to say when you got off, your day off, you went downtown to either do one of three things: find a woman--. And this was in the houses of prostitution. You'd go in the house of prostitution for three minutes, you could have three minutes of sexual activity with a woman. That was looked at sort of not wholly acceptable to not really rejected. It was controlled, I think, by the police department. So you would get in this line. I recall one day that I went with some of my buddies that I worked with at Ford Island. Two of three of them were very, very advanced, when it comes to chasing broads, this type thing. So we were on this truck. The guy who drove was Korean. So we were on this truck, taking furniture into town, and they decided that we were going to stop at this house. I came from Athens, where women were plentiful. I looked at this situation and decided, no, this is not for me. I had never been with a prostitute. Probably spent a lot of time with them later on, but not for a fee. Okay? Night maybe, but no. So I never did. But there were three lines at the time, one for movies, one liquor, and the other was a theater. So when you got in this long line, you would line up in one of three places.
DUNHAM:The theater was motion picture or live theater or music?
GOLDEN: Yeah, movie theater. Movie theater.
DUNHAM:Okay. Did you go to the movies a lot then?
GOLDEN: On your days off, you probably did some of these sort of things. Probably did. But we had other diversions, and we spent a lot of time--. We had gotten to the point where we had a pretty interesting lifestyle.
DUNHAM:How so?
GOLDEN: We were pretty popular. We were pretty popular, because of some of the things that we had learned to do, the manner in which we conducted ourselves.
DUNHAM:The Ten Bachelors had a reputation.
GOLDEN: Yes. Yes, we did.
DUNHAM:Now, since you had a reputation and since you still had some of this informal discrimination, did you ever run into issues--especially because there's a scarcity of women--with white or other guys, other ethnic groups in the community, trying to prevent you from having this social success, if you will?
GOLDEN: My wife, as I said, is fair Portuguese. There were times when we would be walking down the streets of Kalakaua. Kalakaua was not off limits to you; it was just that the hotel and the bars and the cocktail lounges that didn't welcome blacks into it. But up and down, all the way to the beach, or however you wanted to go in Waikiki, there was no reason not to--. No one said, no, you can't do that. But I recall this one time when she and I--I think it was before we got married--we were walking down Kalakaua Avenue. As we walked down, these sailors turned around and started looking, making comments about this black dude walking around with a white woman. Now, my wife didn't know anything about prejudice because she was as open to people as anybody could be. And usually, they were recently here from the mainland. There was a mix. Those who had gone down under, we used to call it, and come back, to them it made no difference. It was usually sailors who'd just come to Hawaii from the mainland, and all of a sudden they decided, hey, look. But they didn't attack you or challenge you, but they made comments. So I answer you in that manner. But it was noticeable. Now, back to this, though. There was a curfew on the buses we would use for transportation. All of the lights, back in those days, automobile lights--those who may have had automobiles--the headlights had a little spot, just one little spot in the center. The rest of it was all blacked out. At ten o'clock, you had to be off the streets. So those of us who were living at Civilian Housing Area 3, or CHA 3, whatever we were doing, [we] knew that we had to be back at CHA 3 before curfew. So we'd all board the bus. I recall one of the nights I was on the bus. The bus was crowded. All of us, mixed, black and white, okay? And maybe some local guys, too. We were going toward CHA 3, about a mile or two from CHA 3. Yeah. Some white and black guys got into a fight. I don't think I was involved in that. Got into a fight. The bus drivers, who were local--. Local guys sort of had a certain empathy for how black people were treated.
DUNHAM:When you say local, you mean Hawaiian?
GOLDEN: Hawaiians. Hawaiian types. Locals. At that time, it was almost indiscriminate. But mostly Hawaiians, I think, were bus drivers. They would leave their bus, as long as the black guys were winning. They would keep the doors closed. As soon as the black guys got somewhere and had won, they'd let them off. But they were on our side. I recall one night this happened. But we had to be back at CHA 3, because of curfew. But there were confrontations, though. You cannot have that mix of men, black and white or any kind of combination, without having some sort of fights, physical things.
DUNHAM:Well, that's why I'm curious, too, about the Ten Bachelors. I hear how successful you were, but I'm just wondering if there was backlash against you, if you will.
GOLDEN: Not us, no. We somehow or other had some--. I don't know what we did; maybe it's the manner--. There's a way you can conduct yourself, that--. I don't recall having to do anything, as far as--. I was possibly, at that age, very able to take care of myself, as far as fights were concerned.
DUNHAM:So you did get into some scrapes? Or no.
GOLDEN: Not that I recall.
DUNHAM:No. Oh, no, okay. Okay, not fights.
GOLDEN: None that I recall. I was a boxer in those days. I boxed in those days. Smokers, they called them. I was a welterweight, and was quite good at it. I also had studied other forms of martial arts. But I didn't have to use it, not even with the husbands of some of those women that I was probably fooling around with.
DUNHAM:Did you compete in boxing, then?
GOLDEN: Yeah. Yeah. But never professionally. I did all the amateur smokers. I did that possibly about a year, and was quite good. They would have those smokers out at CHA 3. One or two of the people that I was in the boxing team with turned pro. But I didn't. What you would do is that you would train rounds, and fight to three rounds, [a] three-round bout. I've come to find out that I was having a constant headache. I decided, wait a minute; if I keep this up, I'll get punch drunk. So I quit.
DUNHAM:That sounds smart.
GOLDEN: I quit. But I recall that I was pretty good. A good friend of mine, who was trying to groom me to turn pro--. At this stage, I said, "Nyet. I don't want to."
DUNHAM:Well, I wanted to go back to, again, where you're knowing you're not welcome at certain places. I guess my question would be, not welcome? Was it not welcome only to blacks? Or was it all non-whites? Were locals and the other Asians and Filipino and Portuguese not welcome, as well? Again, how did you know or navigate that, I guess?
GOLDEN: Can't really answer that entirely. However, this might be one of the answers. At this particular time, there was one man who owned two bars. One was on Smith and King Street, called Roshe's Bar, I think. He owned another bar on Kalakaua Avenue. The one on King and Smith Street catered primarily to country and western, hillbilly-type bar. The one on Kalakaua Avenue had upscale bands--Red Norvo, and all this sort of thing--and this was our hangout. So here's a man that played the game, Barry Buys. I think he was Portuguese, but he played the game, and he had two bars. So the one that he had on Kalakaua Avenue, right near King Street--. I can't [re]call the name for the life of me right now, but it had bigtime bands he brought in quite frequently. So therefore, he was maybe prejudiced in one club and unprejudiced in another. We, the Ten Bachelors, reached a point where we decided that we would host receptions. The highlight of all of these activities is when we hosted the [Nat] King Cole Trio. We hosted them--invitation again, for those guests of ours; I have pictures of that--at Lie H Eyes. Lie H Eyes was [an] upscale Chinese restaurant at the gateway to Waikiki, where we hosted them, and this was quite a coup, quite a thing. We happened to know the promoter. Can't call his name. Japanese guy. He was a friend of ours, too. We made so many friends out here sometimes it escapes me. Ralph Yambuka, that was his name. Ralph Yambuka, he was the promoter, and he brought the King Cole Trio here to McKinley. I think it was McKinley Auditorium. So we were close enough to Ralph, in order to say, listen, let us Ten Bachelors host a reception with the King Cole Trio, and he gave us the opportunity to do it.
DUNHAM:Wow. So I was thinking back to curfew and you show up at nine o'clock.
GOLDEN: Ten o'clock.
DUNHAM:Ten o'clock. Curfew is at ten o'clock. Are you able to have overnight guests? Or did you have overnight guests?
GOLDEN: Good question. I guess so. I suppose so. Because those units, those buildings that we were in for that first contract, were on 18th and 19th Streets, and they were dormitory, what you call. Not quite dormitory. But if you knew more about the layout of the island, I could probably pinpoint some, because they're out near the airport now. There were several individual buildings, with about four units in each. There were two end units, and then the center part had two other units, with up- and downstairs. Each one of those units, I think, had two or three bedrooms and a bath, and I think about four guys were assigned to each one.
DUNHAM:Okay. Okay. Who were your roommates in those?
GOLDEN: In those days? Trying to recall.
DUNHAM:Was it mixed ethnicity?
GOLDEN: No.
DUNHAM:Oh, no. I apologize. You were saying it was all African American. So where were your roommates working? They weren't working the same place you were?
GOLDEN: They worked in Pearl Harbor. One of the guys worked at Pearl Harbor. He was a pipefitter or something like that, making big bucks. I was still at Ford Island.
DUNHAM:How much more do you think he was making?
GOLDEN: I don't know, probably about three or four times what I was making.
DUNHAM:Yeah, yeah. Then after your first contract, did you move from that housing, or was that housing still in effect?
GOLDEN: After the first contract, I took a month's vacation and went to Athens. Then when I returned, I was assigned to the boiler plant. That was a move, a step up.
DUNHAM:When you went to Athens, had you already renewed, or were you thinking it over?
GOLDEN: Yes. That's a good question. That's a good question. I had renewed, but I know when I came back--. By this time, I had learned the ropes and was probably able to work my way into a better assignmnent, or where I wanted to be. So I was assigned to this boiler plant. By and large, eventually, I worked my way up to the boiler plant. The manager of the boiler plant, his son and I were friends. He was from South Carolina. Thomas McQueery. He was liberal. I've been good at this office work sort of thing, so I became his yeoman. That was a term they used in the navy, for one who did office work. On my first contract with the labor crew, yeah, Thomas--. They were from somewhere, North or South Carolina. But I know that Tommy and I were on that labor crew in the beginning, during my first contract. But his dad did run the boiler plant.
DUNHAM:Had you become friends with Tom, Tommy?
GOLDEN: Yeah, we became friends before I went home. Very fine young man. Very, very fine young man. Most of these guys were fantastic. I did not run into any of these guys that I worked with that initial contract, that seemed to have had an attitude. Didn't run into any of that.
DUNHAM:You said you were general laborers. So what was a typical day like for you, during that first contract?
GOLDEN: Cleaning up the yard, cutting grass and that sort of thing. Just outside work.
DUNHAM:Were you always on the day shift?
GOLDEN: Yeah.
DUNHAM:Were there other shifts?
GOLDEN: I don't know. At that time, usually; but the type of work we were doing, we could only do the first and second shifts. They had day shifts, swing shifts, and grave shifts. But that was for more skilled or professional sophisticated-type work. So the jobs that we were doing during that period of time had to be day shifts because that was about the only thing you could do--. But when I returned for my second contract, I worked inside this man's office.
DUNHAM:I wanted to ask you, too, when you first got to Hawaii, I know the island was under martial law and all that, but what was your impression of the island and sort of the ocean and all of that?
GOLDEN: Very, very positive. Very positive. If you recall, I had been on what they call the high seas, the deep indigo colors of the ocean. As I said, for about three days, I was seasick. But then after that, it was still deep, cold blue of the ocean, until you got closer to the islands, I don't know how far from the islands. Then, as you got closer, the waters changed to what you see here, all the various colors--greens and light greens and that sort of thing, warm colors. I knew that this is a place where I wanted to be. It was beautiful. We must've cruised into Pearl Harbor. That's one of the things; I don't recall how we got into Pearl Harbor, but eventually, we must've cruised into Pearl Harbor. But so much, I suppose, was happening that my memory will not recall what my first impressions were, other [than] positive. Mostly positive.
DUNHAM:We haven't talked, since you got to Hawaii, about the friend you came over with. Where is he now? Where was he at that time?
GOLDEN: He came, and there were some of us who came here--. But relationships with the girls back home caused some of us to cancel our contracts. You see, Milton was dating one special girlfriend, a school mate, during our high school days. He and she eventually got married. But when we left, I don't know what happened; she may have gone to college. He went to work somewhere else. He didn't go to work at Ford Island, where I went. I don't know where Milton went to work; he probably went to one of those other places.
DUNHAM:Was he in CH[A] 3, near you?
GOLDEN: Yeah, he lived at CHA 3, but at this time, our shifts and our relationship became somewhat not as close as it had been before we came here. Maybe because of the workplaces that we were in, the friends that we cultivated. But after about six months, the attention for his high school girlfriend took him away. He went back to Athens. Interesting enough, though--he went back, they got married--once you broke that contract, they drafted you. So he was drafted into the Navy. When he came back the next time, although he was married, he was in uniform. So he and some of his Navy friends and I, we used to actually go on the base at Ford Island. We'd put on uniform[s]. I'd put on naval uniforms, and go all around Ford Island, taking advantage of clubs and everything on Ford Island. I don't know what they would've done to me. We probably weren't even thinking about it.
DUNHAM:I was going to say, was there no risk in that?
GOLDEN: Yes. But at nineteen we did weird things. It was stupid.
DUNHAM:Did you ever know anyone who got caught?
GOLDEN: Not that I know, but we didn't.
DUNHAM:Okay. So what were the benefits you got out of that?
GOLDEN: I could go into these clubs they had and get the whisky and stuff they had there. No women, though, unfortunately. But that was the only thing.
DUNHAM:But you already had that mastered, I thought.
GOLDEN: Yeah. Unfortunately, no women. But he came back. Because once you broke that contract, [snaps his fingers] you were in. You were drafted straight away. But he was drafted into the Navy. Now, here's something that's important. I have never--thank God--. Two or three things I'm thankful for. One is I have never had drugs. I've never had a problem with drugs, pot, whatever. None of it. However, what we did, when he and his Navy buddies came back here, we'd go downtown around Smith and Hotel Street. But there was something called a Benzedrex nose inhaler. Inside that nose inhaler was a little piece of cardboard-like thing. So you take that out and you put it in your mouth and you chew on it, and it gave you a high. Right? So when they came back, we went downtown there and they cracked this inhaler open and I went along with it. All of a sudden, I have never felt so stupid in all my life. From then on, it cured me from trying any type of drugs. Some of my friends were into pot; and from pot, they went to coke; and from coke they went to this; and from that, they went and died. But I was saved, by that little incident, and I never wanted to try any form of drugs. Girls I was dating, before we'd go out, they had to have some marijuana. But go ahead. You do your thing, but I don't need it. Never had a need. That's important for me. Other wrongs that [I] may have done. But that's one that I would call saved. No, I don't have that problem. Of all of the things that I have done, all the wrong that I have done-- I'm not saint, and I'm not bragging, but I have to make this point--I have never had an affair with a woman, and she became pregnant and had a child. This is a blessing, because if I had, even during the time before I left Athens, if I had made one of those girls pregnant, I wouldn't have left her. I would've stayed with her, for that child. But I didn't. I don't know how I lucked out, what sort of halo or blessings surrounds me. Thereafter, or maybe because most of the women that I had affairs with were married or older. But I never left not one child behind. Okay.
DUNHAM:We're going to have to change the tape here.
Begin Audiofile 4 07-24-2014.mp3
DUNHAM:We're back. So this is tape four, as we're coming to a close today, here on July 24, 2014. Here, Ernest Golden, and we're still enjoying the outdoors. A little noisy for us, but I think well worth it. Ernest is just going to give us an overview, ground us in some of the dates and some of the significant names, people who can't be here today to share their stories, because we're too late for that. I'll just say that we each have a few other things we want to touch on, before we close for the day. Okay.
GOLDEN: I feel very fortunate that I am in a position now to somehow or another make known those people who I consider played a key role in the society of Hawaii, during the period of 1943 to 1950. I will try to somehow or another give a brief synopsis of what role they played or who they were. I'm going from 1943 to 1950. These were the key players that I knew, many were friends of mine. Daddy Mays. Daddy Mays, at one time, owned a boarding house on King Street. Later became a religious leader. His name should be recorded during that period of time, 1943 to 1950. William Eubanks. We called him Bill Eubanks. Bill Eubanks was from New York, a major from New York, Major Eubanks, when I first met him. I don't know if anyone records the history of Major Eubanks. I met Major Eubanks during this same period of time. He was the first black officer that I had gotten to know. We became friends. Later, he retired here and did some work as a civilian. Lou Wiedman, another friend of mine. Also a name that should be mentioned, because he played a major role here. Robert Clay. Came in the navy, owned a nightclub on Maunakea Street. What's that?
DUNHAM:I don't know, do you want to check? Do you want to pause? I didn't hear. Did you hear something?
GOLDEN: I'm sorry.
DUNHAM:Oh, that's okay. [inaudible voice] Is it okay?
GOLDEN: In that tree.
DUNHAM:Oh, oh. Oh, chopping. Okay. Thank you.
GOLDEN: Al Hooper started out on Two Jacks, on Hotel Street. Al Hooper would greet all of his guests with a special recognition greeting. We night people, whenever we came to Two Jacks, looked for Al Hooper. Al Hooper worked for a man who had another club out at other parts of town, he fired Al, and thought that he could do what Al Hooper did. Al Hooper opened Wonder Bar. Wonder Bar became a very special house for blacks, down on Smith, at Maunakea Street. His name should be recorded in the history of blacks in Hawaii. Alfred Kitt was a friend of mine, and was one of the Ten Bachelors. He worked at Pearl Harbor, as a boiler maker. Then there is Johnny Cunningham, a very good friend of mine. He went back to the mainland and got married. He left, and then later came back because they drafted him. Milton Jordan, who was my best friend, we traveled together throughout our high school years, and then we came to Hawaii together. Milton went back to the mainland, and got married. Eventually came back here, as a sailor. Hampton Brazil, whom I've written about in a book that I co-authored with Delores Guttman. Hampton Brazil was one of the key players. Some brought new services to Hawaii. For an example, James Pate. James Pate was the first person to create bulk baggage delivery to and from Waikiki to the airport. Up until that time, all of the baggage deliver had been done by limousine. James Pate was the forerunner of bulk baggage delivery to and from Waikiki, and Waikiki hotels and the airport. Yoba Corp was a friend of mine. Bernard Wheatley. Bernard Wheatley, I met in 1949, 1950. There's a story of the Hermit of Kauai. That was Bernard Wheatley. I met Bernard Wheatley when he first arrived in the state, and the airport, and I hosted him for a while. Bernard Wheatley's name should be recorded in the history of those who made an impact in Hawaii. Carl Jones came here with the Delta Rhythm Boys. Carl Jones stayed for a while, and he became part of the Hawaii scene. He was a musician. The Delta Rhythm Boys were well-known, at one time. Carl and they broke up here; Carl Jones remained. Carl Jones trained and taught a lot of local musicians the music that he created in his studio. Al Stacy. Al Stacy was one of the original ones in the Civil Service. Al Stacy came to Hawaii before I did. When I arrived in Hawaii, Al Stacy was here. Al Stacy was a chess player. Not checker player. It'll come to me.
DUNHAM:A musical instrument?
GOLDEN: Chess, chess.
DUNHAM:Chess player, okay.
GOLDEN: Chess player. Key chess player, Al Stacy. Al Stacy was here possibly a year before I was. He lived in civilian housing. Stacy and his wife lived on 18th Street. We, the young bachelors and the young single guys of Hawaii, were always seeking those families who were here, the husbands, wives and their children. There were a few of them on 19th Street, and we always tried to, hopefully, become friends, because this was our touch with our roots. Al Stacy was one of those people. Nat Dunson, his wife and his daughter. Nat Dunson was here. He was one of the Prince Hall Masons, and his wife Bertha is still here in Hawaii. Nat Dunson played a major role in the tracks of Hawaii. Frank Williams. Frank Williams came later.
DUNHAM:'43?
GOLDEN: Thank you. 1943 to 1950. There were many others, whose name[s] I have not mentioned, and they should be mentioned. In the civic field, there was William Casey. William Casey was very active as a civic leader. He challenged the local establishment. I don't know when Casey died, but Casey was one of the major leaders in this community. I'm trying to remember, and somehow--.
DUNHAM:Do you recall what kind of things he challenged legally?
GOLDEN: That's a good question.
DUNHAM:Around discrimination?
GOLDEN: It may have been. It may have been. I know that if there was some here, Casey would challenge it. We, at one time--and I think we have to go back to 19--. But we're progressing too far ahead.
DUNHAM:Yeah. We'll come back to the war. I also was wondering, when you talked about hosting people. So I'm thinking, were you actively encouraging others to come from the mainland, African Americans, especially? I know you had your Ten Bachelors groups, but were there other community groups or religious organizations of predominantly blacks, that were kind of a point of contact for newly arrived African Americans?
GOLDEN: None that I know of. I can't think of any. There may have been; I'm not saying there weren't.
DUNHAM:Well, when you mention hosting one of the gentlemen who you just listed, how did that come about, that you hosted him? Sometime soon after his arrival, or from his very arrival?
GOLDEN: He may have been here. He may have been here.
DUNHAM:Oh, okay. It wasn't somebody who came. I was interpreting as somebody, when they got here, you hosted them for a time, while they got acclimated.
GOLDEN: No. No, no, no. Chances are I just becoming involved. There's so much. I'm pushing it a little bit, because I'm so anxious to make sure that these people's names are mentioned. Evidently, a lot of it happened after 1950.
DUNHAM:Well, that's okay. I don't want you to fret today about making sure to list them all. That's why I said I certainly will be happy to, if you want to include a brief addendum when you review the transcript, you'll be more than welcome to do that. Because I understand the importance of this--
GOLDEN: Thank you. Thank you.
DUNHAM:--historical record for you. But I wanted to go back to the war, and I know you have some more to say about it, too. I'm curious. I know that it was prosperous times, and a chance to get away from the Jim Crow, to an extent. There was still some Jim Crow, certainly, going on here. But I'm curious about--. The war is going on; it's certainly prevalent in the consciousness of Hawaii. [airplane noise] Can you hear me okay? Yeah. Sorry. We'll wait a second. But I'm wondering about to what extent there was a sense of patriotism or not--at work, not at work, within your community, within the broader community--what your sense of that is, or mixed feelings. How do you perceive that, during the war?
GOLDEN: I'm not sure if I understand. I'm not sure. Ask me again.
DUNHAM:Was there a strong sense of patriotism. You mentioned going to movies. The movies had the newsreels and all of that. Were you keeping a track of the war? Did you have friends or relatives who did serve?
GOLDEN: Some of my friends were serving. Some of my friends were serving at that time. Some of the names, the guy that came with me. He and two or three of my buddies were actually doing war, during the war. I don't know if we were that consciously aware of anything existing that's notable. Patriotism? Meh. We lived it. I think we lived it, as opposed to saying we focused on it. I don't know if that's reasonable, a reasonable answer or not.
DUNHAM:Oh, everything's a reasonable answer. I'm just trying to get a sense. Did you follow news of the Pacific or in Europe, the war, to the extent--? It's fine if not. I'm just trying to--.
GOLDEN: Make a note.
DUNHAM:Yeah, yeah. Just get a feel for you personally. You doing okay, or you need to take a break? We can take a break.
GOLDEN: We can talk. I can manage this.
DUNHAM:Okay, all right. I'm sorry.
GOLDEN: We can talk.
DUNHAM:We'll try to wrap up here. Well, let me ask about the West Loch Disaster, which again, I don't think you knew about at the time. But it was where you were working, correct, May 21. Was that your birthday?
GOLDEN: Yes, it was my birthday, May 21. This was 1944, a year after I'd been here. So I came in '43, and this is one year, so it's my day off.
DUNHAM:Still within your first eighteen months of service, though, yeah?
GOLDEN: Within the first eighteen months, yes.
DUNHAM:Yeah, okay. Correct.
GOLDEN: I was off that day. I worked at Ford Island, and I was not aware of that tragedy, until Delores Guttman brought it up. West Loch is on the west side of Ford Island, and I worked on the east side?
DUNHAM:Right. Did you work the following Monday, the day after, do you know?
GOLDEN: Yes, I didn't know anything about it, when I came to work, it was just like another routine regular work day.
DUNHAM:Okay. No evidence of a fire or loss of life?
GOLDEN: No, no. No, nothing. Nothing at all, nothing at all of what had happened. Let me share this with you. There was an incident. There was another "incident," there were regular airplane dogfights. One day, as we were traveling, we were delivering furniture from Ford Island to town. We were on Dillingham Boulevard, traveling towards town, when I saw these two planes collide, almost like in slow motion. There's a prison now on Dillingham. One of these planes, after it collided, in slow motion, fell there on Dillingham Boulevard, and burned. Several houses were destroyed. This information was an incident that few knew about.
DUNHAM:So this, like the West Loch, where you worked, and we believe the Navy kind of kept discrete for--.
GOLDEN: Well, it was just something, you didn't discuss it.
DUNHAM:What was the expression you used?
GOLDEN: Loose lips sink ships. This was a slogan during those days. So if it was hush-hush, regardless of whatever it was, it wasn't discussed or talked about, wasn't publicized or anything like that; it was just kept under wraps. So there may have been other incidents that happened similar. My focus has been on some of these things. But I have often said, and to anyone who's listening, is that there were many civilian contributions made throughout this period of time, and throughout most of the time. Most of the writers and those historians have dealt with what they could find in the archives of the University of Hawaii or Bishop Museum. There are possibly others. But since there was nothing recorded elsewhere, whatever battles or whatever events took place down in the Pacific between Hawaii and Japan--may have been many, you see. But like West Loch, who's going to dig up this history? Who's going to bring it? I have no knowledge of it. I'm just saying that if it existed on this island, there may be others. There are other disasters that someone, because of what we're doing, someone may decide, hey, look, I'm going to do some research further, also.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Related to that, had you heard of the Port Chicago explosion from July 17 of '44?
GOLDEN: No.
DUNHAM:At that time, or more recently?
GOLDEN: No, no. More recent.
DUNHAM:No, much more recently.
GOLDEN: Very, very recent. Very recent.
DUNHAM:Okay. You never heard of it at the time.
GOLDEN: No. No, nothing at all. None whatsoever. A lot of things that I read about, as far as the--
DUNHAM:West Loch?
GOLDEN: --Tuskegee, Tuskegee and all that sort of thing that Mrs. [Eleanor] Roosevelt took a role in, most of that came out within the last five years or so, because I've been watching it on TV. But none of this that--. I knew nothing about--.
DUNHAM:So regarding West Loch and that day in May and the aftermath, you did not know of any killed or injured colleagues of yours, or in the housing complex you lived in?
GOLDEN: No. I don't think that any of those civilian workers who I may have worked with or known were part of that. I think, from what I gather after Delores brought this out, is that they were Seabees. The Seabees were some sort of civilian workers that were recruited. How many were blacks, I don't know; but they were civilians. I'm a strange sort of dude. I think we're all sort of cut from the same cloth; it's just which pieces we're cut from, which side. But this big thing about always being different is not worth the time we spend talking about it. So people died. Civilians died, at that period of time. That's what's important.
DUNHAM:Yes. You mean what's the big deal, as far as ethnicity or civilian or military? Yeah. Okay. Fair enough, yeah.
GOLDEN: Yes. Those people I told you about took all that away from me. Then I proceeded further. Because some of the things that I might say to you if we meet again, you might think that I'm losing my cotton-picking mind.
DUNHAM:Well, what you just said made perfect sense to me. I've heard there's a particular interest in remains, in identifying the names. To me, it has maybe more to do with the fact that what you said, loose lips sink ships, and that it was kind of put under wraps, which may have done a disservice, too. I think what you're talking about, about the contributions civilians made.
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:Because there's a duty to honor those who gave their lives, in whatever kind of loss it was, whether it was an accident or--.
GOLDEN: Some may or may not have lost their lives in the process. However, you see, through Delores again--and I have to give her credit for most of this--we have been able to talk, starting out with admirals. There may have been four or five. She's gotten on and made connections with them and gotten an audience with them, and we have been able to talk with them about--. We are trying to get a facility. I'm saying that the reason why--and this is why I'm saying to you, please remember this--that many African Americans in Hawaii don't agree with me, I'm saying that in order to rectify what I consider that mistake that we made by deliberately becoming invisible, we need a fixed location with a fixed schedule. This is my goal. So my whole thing has been on finding a facility which we could have, so that we can hold regular schedule meetings. Presently, we are nomads. Some of my fellow good-intentioned and highly intelligent black folks don't understand that, and they don't agree with the need. But we are going to be forever going around in circles because we don't want to be recognized as an ethnic group. But you don't have to lose your identity. You can maintain it, but still blend into and make your contributions to society and make your contributions to this world in which we live. But you need that something that says I accept who I am. Everybody else realizes who we are. The only thing is, we don't want to realize it. We're black. So be black. Do black things, which you do well. But maintain your ethnicity as your base, because you are--. I'm speaking to my brothers and sister who say, Golden, you're out of your mind. Though you may not want me to do what I'm doing, I won't stop; I'll continue, until I have succeeded. Thank you for giving me the opportunity. But what we need is a facility. Right now, each time Delores has been successful in getting an appointment with an admiral so that we could build a building. I have the nerve to say, listen, you owe us, once using the forty acres a mule approach. Other times I use or call attention to the contributions made, which seem to be the most effective made by civilians to WWII. Therefore, you failed to--and this is my pitch--you failed to recognize civilian contribution. Therefore, you need us. I'm talking to an admiral. You need to use us as that tool, who will rectify that oversight. Now, what do you need to do that, Golden, to rectify the oversight? I need a site. The first one I talked with was Admiral Smith, telling him the same thing I'm saying to you. I said, "That was an oversight. You failed to recognize civilian contributions. You've got all of these military museums. Rightfully so. But you failed to recognize civilian contributions." Admiral Smith's response was, "Okay. We will let you have a building. There's a building for--." We asked him for one out there near Pearl Harbor. He said, "You can't have that building." I'm talking to him like I'm talking to you. He said, "You can't have that building; that's building's occupied." He looked at me like I was a fool. He said, "But we have several, and we will give you one." So then he recommended Little Makalapa? Yeah, I think it was Little Makalapa. He said, "There's four acres over there. It's historical. We can't tear it down, because it's historical. We can't build on it. See what you can do with it." But before he could give us the darn thing--we had to go through all this political stuff, all these various handlers--he was transferred out of Hawaii and went somewhere else. So then we had to go through the whole thing all over [again]. This is what we've been doing for the past eight years. Deloris, the bulldog, is carrying that torch, keeping the quest ongoing. Every time we have an opportunity, Delores is out there knocking on the doors. She's trying to make this happen. She wants a museum; I want the facility. The museum is just part of that bloody facility. She has a museum; she's got all the artifacts. She does everything on an annual basis, like up at Punchbowl Cemetery [National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific]. She does an annual thing up there. She does something else downtown {inaudible}.
DUNHAM:What else do you want the facility for, beyond the museum then?
GOLDEN: I want all of black culture in there. Let's see. Good question. Number one, the museum she has for her artifacts is necessary. I want an administration building, I want a variety shop, I want a theater, restaurant, playground, galleries, radio/TV stations, coffee shop. Not a theater, I want a news thing. I want the radio or cable station coming out of there. I want to be able to broadcast throughout the Pacific Ocean. Broadcasting.
DUNHAM:Yeah. The focus is African American civilian contribution during the war--is that what you're saying? Or is it broader than that?
GOLDEN: It is broader, I want civilian contributions toward the World War II.
DUNHAM:Beyond African American?
GOLDEN: Beyond African American. Because African Americans was just one of them. Not all of them.
DUNHAM:Well, that's why I want you to come to Richmond. I know you don't like to travel so much, but this is where your interview's going to be. It's Rosie the Riveter, but it's World War II Home Front National Historical Park.
GOLDEN: Thank you very much.
DUNHAM:That's where we're going to put yours. So I'm just saying, that's the closest we have to what you've got, and they're doing a great job there.
GOLDEN: You see, let me tell you this. Let me show you this. The NAACP was not found[ed] by blacks only. There was two or three, I think, non-blacks helped form the NAACP. Okay? Now, there've been a whole lot of other Civil Rights movements that wasn't only black. There was a whole lot of white people who died, now. So let's not get carried away with, hey, this is a black thing. No.
DUNHAM:No, I'm just trying to understand.
GOLDEN: No, no, no, but just hear me, because a lot of blacks, they want to think, hey, look at what we did--. No, you didn't. You didn't do this all by yourself. You had help. Even during slavery days, you had help. So this is not my thing. And I don't believe in saying, hey, this is all a black thing. No. I told this organization that I belong to now that if you're saying that this is an all black --. At first, it was Afro American Association, I think. Afro American Association of Hawaii. We had a meeting in or about 2008, and someone in that meeting said, "Well, let's use something like the Hawaiian term for black." But I said to them, "Listen, I will not become a part of an organization that's only focused on black[s]. First of all, I cannot. My wife, my children, my grandchildren are not all black, okay? So I will not go along with this all black thing, because we didn't overcome by ourselves. John Brown, I think, was white. {_________ inaudible}. He wasn't black.
DUNHAM:Okay. That's why I wanted to make sure I heard you.
GOLDEN: No. No, no, no, no. We didn't get this far all alone. And I don't think we have to. I don't think it's necessary.
DUNHAM:Well, I wanted to ask you--. Because thinking back when we first came out, what you talked about, of loss of culture looking back, looking backwards. So do you personally have regrets, or feel that there were forces that were compelling that? Or what is your looking-backwards perspective on that, on that loss of culture that you were lamenting? If I understood you correctly.
GOLDEN: Well, the knowledge, yes. That's a good question. I studied, when I was in high school, under Duval, I studied black history. It was Negro history, I think. Okay. I think we need to know. We aren't the only ones, but I think we need to know something of our culture. We need to know that. Our children need to know it. Now, I talked to my granddaughter. She said, "Yes, there was some study out here at in the school she was attending. I said, 'Are you people doing anything about history, Negro History?'" Yes, I think we should. You need to know that. But you need to know the association of everything that goes along with that--what role you played, what role you didn't play, what role others played with it, where did you come from, and why are you here in the first place.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Well, I'm thinking of, also a parallel with the multiple ethnicities here, but particularly with the Japanese Americans during the war, where there was the massive and need and compulsion to--. Americanization was really accelerated, because of the reality of proving their Americanism.
GOLDEN: Yes.
DUNHAM:And there were newspapers here that played a big role in that, Japanese papers. I'm thinking, you as an African American, along with the many others you've mentioned, were a much smaller minority, so you had similar pressures in another way, I think, that you were speaking to, around sort of Americanization, if you will. It's a little different, because you already--.
GOLDEN: No, I don't know. I'm not quite understanding where you're leading to. I never felt that I was not an American; I just felt that always I've been a black American, which was a hyphenated American. You see, I don't want to be anything else but an American. But I want to be a black American. Okay? I don't want to be a Chinese American, Portuguese American. I just want to be a black American. I love this nation that we were brought to. They brought us here to this nation against our will, scratching and kicking. We want to stay in Africa, but no, they decided, we're going to take you to America. Now you got me over here and I've become American; treat me right.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Yeah, no, absolutely. No, and it's not, for me, more or less American, by any stretch. It's just it's that same thing of being able to retain being a black American, retain your culture; that I was seeing a parallel here in Hawaii, again, with a lot of pressure on Japanese Americans to sort of hide or bury their ethnicity.
GOLDEN: I think it's different. I think it's different. I'm not sure; there might be some similarity. The Japanese had to prove, seemingly, had to prove something, because [of] their relationship with Japan. We don't have that sort of relationship with another nation, so we don't have that baggage to carry.
DUNHAM:No, that's a big difference, there's no question.
GOLDEN: You see, I don't have that baggage.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Well, at any rate, I just wanted to--.
GOLDEN: These are good questions, so don't even bother; ask me and we'll stumble through them, too.
DUNHAM:Yeah. Well, before we close, I want to ask you about this lovely property that I think you first purchased in 1949. You said there was more of a story to what led to that, so I wanted to give you an opportunity. This is the beautiful place we are today. Can you share how that came to be?
GOLDEN: Well, first of all, when my wife would go out and try to find living accommodations, and we went through all of what she'd go through and I realized the racial thing, and the man that once said--. Okay. Then I realized, wait a minute, you're going to raise a family, and this so-called utopia, racial utopia, doesn't exist. So I started out seeking a place where I could raise my family. I didn't want to be at the mercy of landlords. That was my first thought. I did not want to be at the mercy of landlords saying, well, now, we don't want to--. I didn't want to be renting. So I went through the newspaper, and when I saw this article that says that there was land in a place I knew practically nothing about. But the price was something that I could afford. They were asking, oh, 25 cents per square foot.
DUNHAM:1949?
GOLDEN: Yeah. So I went to the office down on Richard Street, across from the post office. I first talked with a Japanese man named Hanou. I responded to the ad. I said, "Well, yes." So he verified that that was the asking price. He said, "Well, come back tomorrow, and I will have drafted up the papers." "What are the terms?" "I want thirty dollars down and thirty dollars a month." Okay. Piece of cake. When I went back the next day, he said, "Are you interested in two lots? His partner was a man named Larry Meadows. He said, "My partner here has another." This was about 24,000; I think the other one was 26,000. Bigger lot. I said, "What are the terms?" He said, "Thirty dollars down, thirty dollars a month." "I'll take them." So sixty dollars down, sixty dollars a month is what I committed myself to. Now, here's where I was saying that somehow or another, this may have been restricted. He said, "I've been told that I shouldn't sell to you." This was Mr. Hanou. Said, "I've been told I shouldn't sell to you." He didn't elaborate. Somehow he said, "Don't make me regret that I'm selling to you." In other words, in so many words, he said, look, I trust you to not say that I made a mistake. But that was it. That was about all he said. That's when I came out and started gathering stones. My brother-in-law is a stone mason. He and I started building. We'd come out on weekends. My wife and her younger brother and I, we'd go up into the mountains over here at Ka'a'awa and gather stones. And with my brother[-in-law], we'd come out here on weekends. Now, I had gone to art school, so I did have knowledge of design, building, stuff like that, so I designed the darn house, based on the knowledge that I had gotten in this art school. When we would come out here, we would camp out here. But you don't go out--. Not in this family, this young family. My wife's young, we had our one child. My brother-in-law and his wife, and then some of others--her sisters and their--we would all gather out here about, oh, a couple of lots over. But we only came out when the weather was nice, okay? So I said, when I was designing the house, that the trade winds came from this way. Like they are now, these trade winds. So I was thinking to myself, if the trades come this direction, the heavy winds will follow, right? These are on nice sunshine-y days, because you don't go camping on rainy days, right? So I designed the house U-shaped. There was no stone house in those days. I had to drive all around the island, trying to find a house made of stone. There's a small town on the other side of the island called Waimanalo, and there was a banker that had a stone house. So I knew nothing about stonework and had no idea of what a stone house would look like, but I did get an idea from that banker, to know what a stone house looks like. I created in my mind. Why I did U-shape, I'm not sure. There's a bedroom on this side and a bedroom on that side. That's a bedroom there. That over there's another bedroom. Two bedrooms. And at each bedroom, from floor to ceiling, is picture window. Okay. We could go take a look. But you can see. They're divided now, but that was one big plate glass. That other bedroom, one big plate glass, facing this bad wind. So when the winds come, heavy winds--whsst--come from this direction. This house here is open. I can stand in one room and look all the way through to the other side. Houses weren't made like this in those days. They had little bedrooms here, a little bedroom there, a little bedroom here, and then a living room and a kitchen, all separate. I opened it up, this house. This is all completely open. They now presently have houses open. It's done now everywhere you turn around, right? Everybody's got an open house. The house there is open; all these houses are open. But the house I designed and built is one of the few homes that was open [back then]. This is add-on. That tile there, that was added on. From that stone over that way is the first unit. We moved into that one when I got enough money to build.
DUNHAM:Yeah. What year was that, about?
GOLDEN: Let me see.
DUNHAM:In the fifties?
GOLDEN: Yeah, that could've been when we moved in. Ooh. Is it '49? Wait, let me get my facts together now.
DUNHAM:Oh, okay. Or you can tell me later.
GOLDEN: Let me get my facts together now, because I got married in 1950. Vangie and I got married in 1950. So it must've been after that. We were married when I started building this house. I may have bought the lot in 1949. No. {_______ inaudible}. Were we married when I built this house? When I bought the lot. Not the house, the lot. Because she was with me when we started gathering the stones. She played a major role. I've got to get some of my facts together.
DUNHAM:Oh, that's all right. Around that time, yeah, yeah.
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah, give or take a year or so.
DUNHAM:Yeah. But it was a big project. Quite beautiful. Quite beautiful. Well, I wanted to go back. Just a couple last things, maybe, on the war years, and if you had anything else to add there. But do you remember V-E Day and V-J Day and the end of the war?
GOLDEN: I remember some things, some of it. If you were out near the airport--. I was out there a week or so ago. The day that the war was declared over, everything just sort of opened up. I was at CHA 3. It was second contract. I think I'd completed the second contract. I know there was a bunker out at CHA 3. A bunker is one of those semi concrete round things that they put out there. They've got some of them out there near the airport now. It must be as wide as this deal here, but it's rounded up to that height--that was round--and possibly fifty feet deep. The end of the war, everybody loudly celebrated. They had these triangles around. I recall going outside and ringing one of the triangles, horns were blowing and all that good stuff, right? I took over that bunker and created an art studio in it. So that gives you an indication of what happened during that time. Everything was relaxed. The beaches, the Royal Hawaiian took the camouflage off of the hotels. Before the war ended, this period of time, you couldn't get to the beaches, because there was barbed wire all along the beach front.
DUNHAM:It was there all the way till the end of the war?
GOLDEN: Yes, to the end of the war. So the beaches were un-accessible. But it was a joyous time. Joyous time.
DUNHAM:Yeah. And your contract went through about a year past the end of the war, then?
GOLDEN: That may be. May be. How long I worked after that--. Some of these so-called facts I'm giving you need a lot of fine tuning.
DUNHAM:That's okay. Sure, sure. Well, did many people lose their jobs near the end of the war, who were working out in that--?
GOLDEN: I don't recall. I do recall one of the things, though. That somewhere during that period of time, wives were able to join their husbands. Up until now, that had not been the thing. There might've been a few exceptions; but by and large, those--.
DUNHAM:In the CH[A] 3 housing?
GOLDEN: In Civilian Housing Area 3. Up until that time--. When I came back the second time, second contract, they had built what we call BQ, bachelor quarters. That was, I think, a three-story building. We had moved. So I had moved off--.
DUNHAM:Out of 'cause[HA] 3?
GOLDEN: First of all, somewhere during this period of time, that second contract--. No. During the Ten Bachelors days, I moved in with a married couples apartment. Once some of the wives and things moved in, they moved down into that housing area. On 18th and 19th Streets, they had built this BQ. But before that, I was living with this family. When I left, when I came back from the mainland, I was living with this family, husband and wife. I was living in their house, in the same type unit that we bachelors had lived in on 18th and 19th Streets.
DUNHAM:Okay. And I didn't clarify. Did the segregation continue through the war, or did that--?
GOLDEN: I think that Truman ended segregation.
DUNHAM:Of the housing?
GOLDEN: I think that he ended segregation in the Navy. I think it was Truman, Harry Truman did it. And that would've ended segregation for housing and everything.
DUNHAM:But yeah, I think that was a little after the end of the war.
GOLDEN: It was, it was.
DUNHAM:Yeah, I think that was more '46 and then--.
GOLDEN: Okay, that's some real information available. You can find this stuff. But I think it was Truman who ended [segregation]. So as the Navy goes, as civilian goes. So we went along with the program.
DUNHAM:Right. Now, I'm curious. I know it wasn't talked about a lot at the time, but were you aware of any gays or lesbians during those years?
GOLDEN: Yeah. But we didn't take na attitude. I let people live. That's your business. That's their business. Now, I think in that BQ quarters that I was in, I know I saw a gay couple making love. I mean guys making love. Worst thing I've seen in my life. Terrible. I don't know, I've written this; I don't care what you do. That's your business. There were some gay people before I left Athens, in the church. There was a guy who was active in the church. Oh, there were more than that. Around where I grew up, I don't know, we didn't make an issue of it. But I grew up knowing that there were some lesbians, yeah. Gay people? Yeah. Funny, though. We didn't grow up ostracizing them. We didn't. I don't know, it's a funny thing. I've never given it that much thought. Never really gave it that much thought. I came from a family that seemed to live and let live.
DUNHAM:Okay. Seems like it, yeah.
GOLDEN: That seemed to be how we were. Right now, I can care less.
XiYeah. Yeah. Well, before we close, what have I not asked you about today that you would like to tell us about? Any last thoughts?
GOLDEN: I'm not sure. I think you've let me spill my guts pretty good. I do feel, though, that this need for a location is paramount in my thinking, because it is the solution to the problems that we have. And I think with that facility, we can bring about so much, so many positive things that's needed in this community. This is my goal. Why? I'm not sure. I could quit. I have three things that I love doing, and I've committed myself to do this. I want my music, my art, and my writing. We might end my writing now. If I can get this completed, I don't know if I want to write anything else or not. Maybe.
DUNHAM:Well, it's always good to keep writing. Let me, just before we close, ask you about music, and we'll wrap up. Because I know at the beginning, you said music's tied to memories. Are there any other strong memories, or your own music that you continue to make today, that you wanted to share with us about? I know in other interviews, I read of you seeing Billie Holiday, Paul Robeson. You mentioned the King Cole Trio, hosting them. Is there anything else you'd like to touch on about music you've seen or music you've made yourself?
GOLDEN: Oh, no. My son Carl wants me to write something, sing a song he has written, but I'm not sure that I can do it. I've listened to the song, and I've somehow decided maybe I would write one number. I've listened to all these other people who've written music, trying to get an idea of what they're doing. I guess it's all right. But unless I can do something that's meaningful and different, I'll be out here still trying to somehow satisfy his request.
DUNHAM:But you're still performing and singings songs, and you sing predominantly songs from the thirties and forties, from this early era of yours.
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah. To a certain extent. But I do country; I do some country things. The blind guy--and I can't call his name right now--.
DUNHAM:Blind country artist?
GOLDEN: Yeah, yeah. Ronnie Milsap.
DUNHAM:Living?
GOLDEN: I think so. It'll come to me. But I do one of his numbers, and I do some of the other things. I try to incorporate some of the things that I went through back in my high school and early years, that's non-bluesy. I'm Bill Blues, but I try to do a cross-section of other things, and somehow or another seem to be successful in it. We had a very successful session out at the polo grounds about a week and a half ago, we've been asked to come back again. Maybe I told you, but we played on afternoon. We went on about five o'clock. Yeah, about this time in the afternoon. We had them dancing, dancing on the grassy sides of the field track. There must've been about 300 or so people.
DUNHAM:Great.
GOLDEN: I had certain hesitations, apprehensions about how we were going to be accepted. The young lady--who's a good friend of my son's, was so gracious in setting us up, making it possible--treated me as if I was somebody. We had a special table and we watched all the polo races and stuff. It was a beautiful afternoon and evening. A very beautiful evening. The lady that sponsors the whole thing liked what I was doing. I did most of the vocals. Now, sometimes my son does some vocals; Kelly does some vocals. Kelly's the one that called me a while ago. Kelly does some vocals. Both of them are very talented. They could get along without me.
DUNHAM:Well, that being said, I don't know if you have much voice left, but is there any bit of a song you might like to close with today? Is there anything that would be fitting?
GOLDEN: I can't think of anything. Can't think of anything.
DUNHAM:Oh, okay. Well, gee. I'm thinking, all these songs from the thirties and forties, there must be one. All right, well, we'll let you [go].
GOLDEN: Maybe another time.
DUNHAM:All right. Well, it was very beautiful, what you shared with us before and the whole story.
GOLDEN: Thank you so much.
DUNHAM:So thank you so much.
[End of Interview]
